packs.name: Free Draw Studio
packs.description: Open-ended creative challenges. Express yourself, discover math through art, and push the robot to its limits.

challenges.title: First Doodle
challenges.subtitle: Let the robot draw anything
challenges.description: No instructions, no rules — just ask SketchBot to draw something and see what it creates. Your first creative conversation.
learning_objectives: Send a drawing prompt to a robot
Observe how AI interprets natural language

steps.tutor_message: Hi! I'm Sketch. I can draw almost anything — animals, landscapes, symbols, abstract shapes. What should I draw first? Type anything you like!
steps.student_prompt: Type what you'd like me to draw.

steps.tutor_message: Here we go!

payload.prompt: {{student_input}}

steps.tutor_message: What do you think? Did I draw what you imagined, or something different? Try describing it differently and see if my next drawing changes.
steps.reflection_question: What words would make your prompt more specific so I draw exactly what you picture?

completion_badge.name: First Stroke
completion_badge.description: Sent the first drawing prompt

challenges.title: Shape Alphabet
challenges.subtitle: Build letters from geometric shapes
challenges.description: Every letter in the alphabet can be built from lines, circles, and arcs. Can you design one?
learning_objectives: Recognize geometric primitives in everyday objects
Decompose complex shapes into parts

steps.tutor_message: Look at the letter 'A'. It's two diagonal lines and one horizontal line in the middle. Every letter is secretly a geometry problem! Which letter do you want to build?
steps.reflection_question: What basic shapes (lines, curves, circles) make up your chosen letter?

steps.tutor_message: Great pick! I'll draw your letter using only geometric shapes — no cheating with fancy fonts.

payload.prompt: the letter {{student_input}} drawn large and clearly using only straight lines and circular arcs, centered on the page

steps.tutor_message: Count the segments in my drawing. How many lines? How many curves? That's how many basic commands I needed.
steps.reflection_question: Which letter do you think would need the fewest geometric pieces? Which would need the most?

completion_badge.name: Type Designer
completion_badge.description: Built a letter from geometry

challenges.title: Silhouette Sketch
challenges.subtitle: Draw the outline of an animal
challenges.description: A silhouette captures the essential shape of something with a single unbroken line. Choose an animal and see how little detail is needed to recognize it.
learning_objectives: Understand contour drawing
Identify key features that make a shape recognizable

steps.tutor_message: I'm going to draw a silhouette — just an outline, no details inside. What animal do you want? Pick something with a distinctive shape like a cat, elephant, or shark.
steps.student_prompt: Name an animal for the silhouette.

steps.tutor_message: Drawing the silhouette now — just one clean outline!

payload.prompt: a simple clean silhouette outline of a {{student_input}}, single continuous stroke, no internal details

steps.tutor_message: Show the drawing to someone nearby without telling them the animal. Can they guess what it is? A good silhouette only needs the most important curves.
steps.reflection_question: What one feature makes your animal instantly recognizable — ears, tail, beak?

completion_badge.name: Contour Artist
completion_badge.description: Captured an animal in pure outline

challenges.title: Zone Map
challenges.subtitle: Divide the page into regions
challenges.description: Artists and mapmakers both think in zones. Design a page layout that divides the paper into distinct areas — then fill each with a different pattern.
learning_objectives: Understand area and partitioning
Combine geometric shapes to fill a space

steps.tutor_message: Imagine the page as a city map — different districts, each with its own character. Tell me how many zones you want and any shapes you have in mind: circles, triangles, stripes?
steps.student_prompt: Describe your zone layout (e.g., '4 quadrants' or '3 circles inside each other').

steps.tutor_message: Laying out your zones now — I'll fill each one with a different hatching or dot pattern so they're easy to tell apart.

payload.prompt: a page divided into {{student_input}} zones, each filled with a distinct repeating pattern: hatching, dots, cross-hatch, or waves

steps.tutor_message: Which zone has the most ink on it? Which has the least? Artists use this to guide the viewer's eye — busy zones grab attention first.
steps.reflection_question: If this were a real map, what would each zone represent?

completion_badge.name: Zone Planner
completion_badge.description: Designed a multi-zone page layout

challenges.title: Pattern Tile
challenges.subtitle: Design a repeating tile
challenges.description: Wallpaper, fabric, and floor tiles all use one simple trick: repeat a small pattern endlessly. Design a tile and let SketchBot fill the page with it.
learning_objectives: Understand tiling and repetition
Recognize translational symmetry

steps.tutor_message: A tile pattern starts with one small unit that can repeat without gaps or overlaps. It could be a star, a diamond, a leaf — anything that can tessellate. What's your tile?
steps.student_prompt: Describe the shape for your repeating tile.

steps.tutor_message: Stamping your tile across the whole page now!

payload.prompt: a seamless repeating tile pattern filling the full page, based on {{student_input}} shapes arranged in a grid with no gaps

steps.tutor_message: Look at the full pattern — can you still see individual tiles, or does it look like one continuous texture? That's the magic of a good tile.
steps.reflection_question: How would the pattern change if you rotated each tile 45°?

completion_badge.name: Pattern Maker
completion_badge.description: Designed a repeating tile pattern

challenges.title: Robot Story Strip
challenges.subtitle: A 3-panel comic about a robot
challenges.description: Every comic strip tells a story in three frames: setup, conflict, resolution. Script a 3-panel robot adventure and let SketchBot illustrate it.
learning_objectives: Understand narrative structure
Communicate ideas visually across panels

steps.tutor_message: Every great story needs three beats: something starts, something goes wrong (or surprising), then it resolves. What happens in your robot story? Give me three sentences — one per panel.
steps.student_prompt: Write three sentences for your comic strip panels.

steps.tutor_message: Drawing your 3-panel strip now. Each panel will be a simple scene in a bordered box.

payload.prompt: a 3-panel comic strip with bordered boxes: panel 1 — {{panel_1}}, panel 2 — {{panel_2}}, panel 3 — {{panel_3}}, simple clear line art

steps.tutor_message: Show it to someone — do they understand the story without you explaining it? Good visual storytelling doesn't need words.
steps.reflection_question: What would you add or change in the middle panel to make the conflict more dramatic?

completion_badge.name: Storyteller
completion_badge.description: Wrote and illustrated a robot comic

challenges.title: Spirograph
challenges.subtitle: Circular motion makes complex curves
challenges.description: A spirograph is a circle rolling inside another circle — the pen traces a path called a hypotrochoid. Change the ratio of radii and the whole pattern transforms.
learning_objectives: Understand parametric equations
See how simple rules produce complex patterns
Explore ratio relationships

steps.tutor_message: A spirograph has two numbers: the outer radius R and the inner radius r. The ratio R/r determines how many petals appear. Try R=5, r=3 — that gives a 5-petal flower. Change r to 2 and it becomes 5 loops. What ratio do you want to try?
steps.student_prompt: Choose R (outer, e.g. 5) and r (inner, e.g. 3).

steps.tutor_message: Calculating the hypotrochoid path and drawing it now — watch the pattern emerge!

payload.prompt: a hypotrochoid spirograph with outer radius {{R}} and inner radius {{r}}, centered on the page, single continuous line

steps.tutor_message: How many lobes did you get? It should equal R — gcd(R,r)... divided into some whole number. Try a different ratio and predict the petal count before I draw.
steps.reflection_question: What happens when R and r share no common factors (like 5 and 7)? How many times does the pen have to loop before the pattern closes?

completion_badge.name: Curve Explorer
completion_badge.description: Generated a hypotrochoid spirograph

challenges.title: Pixel Art
challenges.subtitle: Draw on a grid like early video games
challenges.description: Early video games had tiny screens — 8×8 or 16×16 pixels. Every character had to be designed on a grid. Design yours and let SketchBot render it full-size.
learning_objectives: Understand raster graphics and pixel coordinates
Design within constraints
Connect art to computer graphics fundamentals

steps.tutor_message: I'm going to draw a 16×16 grid on the paper first. Each square is one 'pixel'. You'll describe which pixels to fill in — or just tell me what the image should be and I'll design it.
steps.student_prompt: What should the pixel art image be? (Or describe a pattern)

steps.tutor_message: Rendering your pixel art on the grid now — each filled square gets a solid black fill.

payload.prompt: a 16x16 pixel art grid on the page with a {{student_input}} design, each pixel is a small filled square, clean grid lines

steps.tutor_message: Step back a few feet from the paper. Can you see the image more clearly from a distance? That's exactly how a screen works — your eye blends the squares together.
steps.reflection_question: Original Game Boy had a 160×144 pixel screen. How many of your 16×16 tiles would fit on it?

completion_badge.name: Pixel Artist
completion_badge.description: Created pixel art on a drawn grid

challenges.title: Mathematical Rose
challenges.subtitle: r = cos(n·θ) — petals from an equation
challenges.description: The equation r = cos(nθ) in polar coordinates draws a perfect rose. One number — n — controls everything: even n gives 2n petals, odd n gives n petals.
learning_objectives: Understand polar coordinates
Connect trigonometric functions to geometry
Explore even/odd petal rule

steps.tutor_message: In polar coordinates, a point is described by (r, θ) — distance from center and angle. The equation r = cos(nθ) traces a path as θ sweeps from 0° to 360°. When n=3, you get 3 petals. When n=4, you get 8! Try n=5.
Plotting r = cos({{n}}·θ) from 0 to 2π now.

payload.prompt: a mathematical rose curve r = cos({{n}} * theta) in polar coordinates, centered on the page, clean single-line drawing, {{n}}-petal rose if odd or {{n*2}}-petal if even

steps.tutor_message: Count the petals! Did you get n petals (for odd n) or 2n petals (for even n)? Now predict: what will n=6 look like before I draw it?
steps.reflection_question: Try r = sin(nθ) instead of cos — how does that change the orientation of the rose?

completion_badge.name: Polar Explorer
completion_badge.description: Drew a polar rose with trigonometry

challenges.title: Tessellation
challenges.subtitle: Tile the plane — no gaps, no overlaps
challenges.description: M.C. Escher filled pages with interlocking lizards and birds. The secret is a shape that tiles perfectly. There are only three regular polygons that can tile a plane — can you name them?
learning_objectives: Identify which regular polygons tessellate
Understand interior angle sum at a vertex
Recognize Escher-style transformation tilings

steps.tutor_message: For a shape to tile with no gaps, its interior angles must add up to exactly 360° at every meeting point. A triangle has 60° angles — six fit perfectly. A square has 90° — four fit. A hexagon has 120° — three fit. A pentagon has 108° — try fitting three: 108×3 = 324°. Gap!
steps.reflection_question: Why can't regular pentagons tile a flat plane?

steps.tutor_message: Drawing a hexagonal honeycomb tessellation — the most efficient tiling in nature.

payload.prompt: a regular hexagonal tessellation (honeycomb) filling the full page, all hexagons equal size, no gaps, clean line art

steps.tutor_message: Escher modified tiles by adding bumps and notches that interlock — so adjacent tiles 'grip' each other like puzzle pieces. Imagine cutting a notch from one side and adding it to the opposite side of a square. The area stays the same, but it looks like an animal!
steps.reflection_question: Sketch on paper: take a square tile and make one modification so it could look like a bird or fish when repeated.

completion_badge.name: Plane Filler
completion_badge.description: Explored the math of tessellation

challenges.title: Motion Trail
challenges.subtitle: Visualize a moving path as a drawing
challenges.description: Animators use motion trails to show speed and direction. Program a sequence of moves and let the pen trace the robot's path — the drawing IS the data.
learning_objectives: Represent motion as a data trace
Understand velocity and direction changes in a visual medium

steps.tutor_message: I'm going to move around the paper while the pen is down. You write the choreography: a sequence of moves like 'forward 10cm, turn right 45°, forward 5cm…' — the drawing will record exactly where I went.
steps.student_prompt: Write a sequence of moves (forward, backward, turn left/right with distances and angles).

steps.tutor_message: Executing your choreography now — the pen records every step.

payload.prompt: a continuous motion trail path based on the following movement sequence: {{student_input}}, drawn as a single pen trace on the page

steps.tutor_message: Look at the trail. Where are the corners? Where does it look like I was going fast vs slow? You can read a robot's behavior from its ink path.
steps.reflection_question: What sequence of moves would draw a perfect spiral outward from the center?

completion_badge.name: Path Tracer
completion_badge.description: Visualized a robot motion sequence as a drawing

challenges.title: Bézier Curves
challenges.subtitle: Smooth curves from control points
challenges.description: Every font, car body, and animation path uses Bézier curves. Two anchor points and one or two control points — pull the curve like a rubber band.
learning_objectives: Understand linear interpolation and De Casteljau's algorithm
Relate control points to curve shape
Recognize Bézier curves in real-world design

steps.tutor_message: A quadratic Bézier curve has three points: P0 (start), P1 (control), P2 (end). At time t, you interpolate between P0→P1 to get Q, and P1→P2 to get R, then interpolate Q→R. Sweep t from 0 to 1 and you trace the curve. It's recursive interpolation — elegant!
steps.reflection_question: If P1 is exactly between P0 and P2, what does the curve look like? What if P1 is very far to one side?

steps.tutor_message: Drawing a cubic Bézier art composition — multiple curves flowing together like a ribbon.

payload.prompt: an abstract composition of flowing cubic Bezier curves, smooth S-curves and loops, balanced across the page, single-line elegant art

steps.tutor_message: Font designers place control points to shape every letter. The letter 'S' is two Bézier curves connected. Try sketching control points on paper for one letter, then I'll draw it.
steps.student_prompt: Describe where to place the control points for a letter of your choice.
steps.reflection_question: Why do professional designers prefer Bézier curves over freehand paths for logos?

completion_badge.name: Curve Sculptor
completion_badge.description: Mastered control-point curve design

challenges.title: Lissajous Figures
challenges.subtitle: Two sine waves collide on paper
challenges.description: When you combine two sine waves at right angles — one for X, one for Y — the robot traces a Lissajous figure. Change the frequency ratio and a completely different shape emerges.
learning_objectives: Understand parametric motion from two oscillations
Relate frequency ratio to curve complexity
Connect to oscilloscope Lissajous patterns in electronics

steps.tutor_message: x(t) = A·sin(a·t + δ), y(t) = B·sin(b·t). The ratio a:b decides everything. 1:1 = ellipse, 1:2 = figure-8, 2:3 = a pretzel, 3:5 = a complex knot. The phase δ rotates the whole figure. What ratio do you want?
steps.student_prompt: Choose frequency ratio a:b (e.g. 1:2, 3:4, 2:5) and a phase angle δ (0, 45, 90).

steps.tutor_message: Plotting x = sin({{a}}·t), y = sin({{b}}·t + {{delta}}°) as t goes from 0 to 2π.

payload.prompt: a Lissajous figure with frequency ratio {{a}}:{{b}} and phase shift {{delta}} degrees, centered on the page, single closed or open curve

steps.tutor_message: Oscilloscopes display voltage vs voltage — exactly a Lissajous figure. Technicians use it to measure the frequency ratio between two signals by counting loops. How many loops cross the vertical axis in your drawing?
steps.reflection_question: The loop count on the vertical axis equals the ratio 'a'. Does your drawing confirm this?

completion_badge.name: Wave Artist
completion_badge.description: Combined two sine waves into art

challenges.title: Fourier Sketch
challenges.subtitle: Approximate any shape with sine waves
challenges.description: Any shape can be approximated by adding up sine waves of different frequencies — this is Fourier synthesis. Add more waves, get more detail. This is how MP3s and JPEGs work.
learning_objectives: Understand superposition of waves
See how frequency components build a waveform
Connect to audio/image compression concepts

steps.tutor_message: Start with one sine wave — a smooth bump. Add a second at 3× the frequency (1/3 the amplitude): the wave gets steeper. Add a 5th harmonic: steeper again. By the time you've added ~20 harmonics, you have a nearly perfect square wave. This is Fourier series!
steps.reflection_question: Why would you want to represent a square wave as a sum of smooth sine waves?

steps.tutor_message: Drawing the first 5 harmonics of a square wave superimposed — you'll see the Gibbs ringing phenomenon at the edges.

payload.prompt: a graph showing the Fourier series approximation of a square wave using 5 harmonics — show each sine wave individually and their sum as a thicker line, labeled axes

steps.tutor_message: JPEG images compress photos by storing only the most important Fourier components (using cosines). A low-quality JPEG has fewer components — that's why it looks blocky. The blockiness IS the Gibbs phenomenon you just saw.
steps.reflection_question: Audio compression (MP3) removes high-frequency components you can barely hear. What are the tradeoffs of removing too many?

completion_badge.name: Harmonic Engineer
completion_badge.description: Built a shape from sine waves

challenges.title: Fractal Tree
challenges.subtitle: Recursion draws a forest
challenges.description: A fractal tree is defined by one rule repeated forever: split each branch into two smaller branches at an angle. The result looks exactly like a real tree — because real trees use the same rule.
learning_objectives: Understand recursive algorithms
Recognize self-similarity in nature
Tune branching parameters to change tree shape

steps.tutor_message: Recursion means a function that calls itself. Draw a trunk. At the top, draw two branches each at 30° from vertical, each 70% as long as the trunk. Then repeat for each branch. After 8 levels, you have 2^8 = 256 twigs — from one simple rule.
steps.student_prompt: Choose: branch angle (10–45°), length ratio (50–80%), and number of levels (4–9).

steps.tutor_message: Running the recursive branching algorithm now — {{levels}} levels deep.

payload.prompt: a fractal binary tree: trunk from bottom center, each branch splits into two at {{angle}} degrees, each child is {{ratio}}% the length, {{levels}} recursive levels, detailed fine lines

steps.tutor_message: Real trees use a similar algorithm — with randomness added. The branching angle, ratio, and number of splits vary slightly each time, giving organic irregularity. Your tree is deterministic; a real tree is stochastic.
steps.reflection_question: How would you add randomness to make the tree look more natural? What parameter would you randomize?

completion_badge.name: Recursive Thinker
completion_badge.description: Generated a fractal tree from a simple rule

challenges.title: 3D on Paper
challenges.subtitle: Project a 3D surface onto 2D
challenges.description: 3D graphics engines project 3D points onto a 2D screen using matrix math. SketchBot can do the same — project a 3D wireframe onto the flat paper.
learning_objectives: Understand 3D→2D projection matrices
Visualize orthographic vs perspective projection
Implement rotation and projection by hand

steps.tutor_message: To draw a 3D point (x,y,z) on paper: in orthographic projection, just drop z → draw (x,y). In perspective, divide by z: draw (x/z, y/z). Perspective makes far things smaller — orthographic doesn't. Which would you use for architecture drawings? For a map?
steps.reflection_question: Why does perspective projection require dividing by z, and what happens when z approaches 0?

steps.tutor_message: Drawing a rotating wireframe cube projected onto the paper — I'll show it at three different rotation angles side by side.

payload.prompt: three wireframe cubes in a row, each rotated 30° more around the Y-axis, drawn using perspective projection with vanishing point, clean line art, visible hidden edges dashed

steps.tutor_message: Every rotation is a 3×3 matrix multiplication. Rx rotates around X, Ry around Y, Rz around Z. Chain them together and you rotate in any direction. Game engines do this millions of times per second for every vertex on screen.
steps.reflection_question: What happens to the projection when you rotate the cube exactly 90° — can you predict which faces disappear?

completion_badge.name: Dimension Bender
completion_badge.description: Projected 3D geometry onto 2D paper

challenges.title: L-System Plants
challenges.subtitle: Grammar rules grow a plant
challenges.description: Lindenmayer systems (L-systems) use string-rewriting rules to model plant growth. Start with 'F', apply rules like F→FF+F, and interpret the result as drawing commands. Nature in code.
learning_objectives: Understand formal grammars and string rewriting
Implement a turtle-graphics interpreter
Model biological growth computationally

steps.tutor_message: An L-system has: an axiom (starting string), production rules (replacements), and an interpretation (F = draw forward, + = turn right, - = turn left, [ = save position, ] = restore). Axiom: F. Rule: F → F[+F]F[-F]F. After 3 iterations this becomes a branching plant. Want to try it?
steps.student_prompt: Choose iteration depth (2–5) and turn angle (15–45°). Or write your own rule.

steps.tutor_message: Expanding the L-system grammar {{depth}} times and drawing the result as turtle graphics.

payload.prompt: an L-system plant drawing using {{depth}} iterations of the rule F→F[+F]F[-F]F, turn angle {{angle}} degrees, starting from the bottom center, branching plant silhouette, fine lines

steps.tutor_message: The paper 'Algorithmic Beauty of Plants' by Prusinkiewicz (1990) used L-systems to model hundreds of plant species — each with slightly different rules. The same math that grows a fern also grows a coral. Biological growth IS computation.
steps.reflection_question: How would you modify the rule to make the plant grow wider at the base and thinner at the top?

completion_badge.name: Botanical Programmer
completion_badge.description: Grew a plant with a grammar rule

packs.name: Sumo Arena
packs.description: Design and program a sumo robot — master pushing, edge detection, and real-time strategy.

challenges.title: Meet the Ring
challenges.subtitle: Rules of the sumo arena
challenges.description: Before your robot fights, you need to know the battlefield. Learn the ring's layout, the white edge line, and what it means to lose.
learning_objectives: Understand sumo competition rules
Identify the ring boundary and its detection role
Define win/loss conditions

steps.tutor_message: In robot sumo, the arena is a black circle with a white ring around the edge. Your robot wins by pushing the opponent out — but if any part of your robot crosses the white line, YOU lose. The white line is both a weapon and a trap.
steps.reflection_question: Why is the white line at the EDGE rather than in the center? What strategic role does it play?

steps.tutor_message: Sumo robots usually have two types of sensors: line sensors underneath (detect the white edge) and range sensors in front (detect the opponent). Let's test the line sensors by slowly driving to the edge. Watch what happens when I hit white.

payload.prompt: a top-down diagram of a sumo ring: black circle, white border ring, robot position in center, arrows showing sensor detection zones (front: rangefinder, bottom: line sensor)

steps.tutor_message: The diagram shows two detection zones. A real sumo robot reacts to the line sensor BEFORE the wheel crosses it — it needs to be a tiny bit ahead. That's called a safety margin.
steps.reflection_question: If the line sensor is 3cm from the front wheel and the robot moves at 0.5m/s, how many milliseconds does the code have to react before a wheel crosses the line?

completion_badge.name: Ring Scholar
completion_badge.description: Learned the sumo arena rules

challenges.title: The Push Test
challenges.subtitle: Measure force and traction
challenges.description: Sumo is about force — but not just motor power. Grip, weight distribution, and contact surface all matter. Test your robot's pushing force and discover what limits it.
learning_objectives: Understand friction and normal force
Measure traction vs motor torque
Identify the limiting factor in a push

steps.tutor_message: Friction force = μ × Normal force (weight). If your robot weighs 500g and μ (rubber on mat) is 0.7, max friction = 0.7 × 4.9N = 3.43N. If your motors can push harder than that, the wheels slip — not a motor problem, a traction problem!
steps.reflection_question: Would adding weight to the robot help or hurt in sumo? When would more weight make things worse?

steps.tutor_message: Drive forward against a fixed wall and measure how far the wheels slip. A kitchen scale between the robot and wall shows the actual push force. What did you measure vs what the motors should produce?
steps.student_prompt: Place the robot against a wall and measure push force with a scale. Record your result.

steps.tutor_message: Most beginners think 'more power = better sumo'. But once wheels slip, extra power does nothing. The real limit is grip × weight. Lower center of mass and wider wheels help more than a stronger motor.
steps.reflection_question: Name two ways to increase friction force without changing the motor.

completion_badge.name: Force Tester
completion_badge.description: Measured robot pushing force and traction

challenges.title: Hold Your Ground
challenges.subtitle: Stay in the ring under pressure
challenges.description: Even without an opponent, a sumo robot must detect and avoid the edge. Write a rule that keeps the robot on the black surface at all times.
learning_objectives: Write a reactive edge-avoidance rule
Understand event-driven programming
Test and tune a safety response

steps.tutor_message: The simplest safe rule: IF line sensor detects white → immediately reverse and rotate 180°. This is called a reflex — it triggers regardless of what the robot was doing before. Write this rule using the NL Rules editor.
steps.student_prompt: Use the Rules editor: trigger = line_detected, action = reverse_and_turn_180.

steps.tutor_message: Now I'll drive forward and slowly approach the edge. When the line sensor fires, the escape rule should kick in. Let's test it 5 times from different angles.

payload.prompt: a top-down diagram showing 5 escape trajectories from the ring edge — approach from different angles, each showing the reverse-and-rotate recovery path in dashed lines

steps.tutor_message: Did the escape always work? If the robot was moving fast, it might slide past the line before the rule activates. The fix: detect the line earlier (lower threshold) or reduce speed near the edge.
steps.reflection_question: What's the tradeoff between reacting earlier vs reacting aggressively to an edge detection?

completion_badge.name: Ring Holder
completion_badge.description: Programmed reliable edge avoidance

challenges.title: Edge Awareness
challenges.subtitle: Predict and react before it's too late
challenges.description: Detecting the edge after you're on it is too late. Real sumo robots detect the WHITE before the wheel reaches it. Add predictive edge logic to your controller.
learning_objectives: Understand sensor placement relative to wheel contact
Design a predictive safety margin
Implement multi-sensor edge detection

steps.tutor_message: If the front sensor is 4cm ahead of the front wheel, and the robot moves at 40cm/s, the sensor gives you 100ms warning. At 80cm/s (full speed), only 50ms. You need to either slow down near edges OR place sensors further ahead.
steps.reflection_question: A robot 15cm long wants a 200ms reaction time at full speed (60cm/s). Where should the front sensor be placed?

steps.tutor_message: Two line sensors (left and right front corners) help determine which way to turn. Left sensor hits first → turn right. Right sensor first → turn left. This gives smarter escapes than always turning the same direction.

payload.prompt: a diagram of a sumo robot's underside showing left and right front line sensors, with arrows showing which direction to turn based on which sensor triggers first

steps.tutor_message: Run your robot in the arena and approach the edge from 6 different angles. Log which sensor fires first each time. Does the turn direction always make sense?
steps.student_prompt: Test 6 edge approaches and record which sensor fires first and the resulting escape direction.
steps.reflection_question: What happens when BOTH sensors fire simultaneously (approaching straight-on)? What should the robot do?

completion_badge.name: Edge Reader
completion_badge.description: Implemented predictive multi-sensor edge detection

challenges.title: First Bout
challenges.subtitle: Your first real match
challenges.description: Combine edge avoidance with basic opponent-seeking. Your robot should search for the opponent, drive toward it, and push — while never stepping out of the ring.
learning_objectives: Integrate multiple behaviors (search + push + escape)
Understand state-machine architecture
Observe emergent behavior from simple rules

steps.tutor_message: Your robot needs three states: SEARCH (rotate slowly, scanning), ATTACK (drive toward detected opponent), and ESCAPE (react to line sensors). These three simple states, combined, create a complete sumo strategy.
steps.reflection_question: What triggers the transition from SEARCH to ATTACK? What triggers ESCAPE to override ATTACK?

steps.tutor_message: Build the state machine using the Blocks editor — three behavior blocks, each with entry conditions. Connect them with transition arrows: range_sensor < 40cm → ATTACK, line_detected → ESCAPE, timer_expired in ATTACK → SEARCH.
steps.student_prompt: Build the 3-state machine in Blocks and run a test match.

steps.tutor_message: Watch the match replay. How long did the robot spend in each state? Too much SEARCH time means slow opponent acquisition. Too quick ATTACK transitions mean false positives. Watch for the pattern.
steps.reflection_question: What was the most common failure mode? The robot stepping out, losing track, or getting stuck in SEARCH?

completion_badge.name: Ring Warrior
completion_badge.description: Completed the first real sumo match

challenges.title: Sumo Tactics
challenges.subtitle: Outsmart, not just outpush
challenges.description: Top sumo robots use strategy: flanking, feinting, and edge-trapping. Study three classic tactics and design a counter-strategy.
learning_objectives: Analyze real sumo robot strategies
Understand the attacker vs defender tradeoff
Design a counter-strategy for a known opponent

steps.tutor_message: Three classic tactics: (1) RUSH — full speed at start, slam before opponent can react. (2) FLANK — approach from the side where sensors are weakest. (3) EDGE-TRAP — push toward the nearest edge rather than center. Which does your current robot defend against best?
steps.reflection_question: Your robot rotates to find the opponent. A RUSH bot is already moving before your scan completes. How do you defend against that?

steps.tutor_message: Let me diagram the three attack vectors and their optimal counter-positions.

payload.prompt: a top-down sumo ring diagram showing 3 attack patterns: rush (straight arrow from far edge), flank (curved approach from side), edge-trap (angled push toward white line) with defender counter-positions marked

steps.tutor_message: Counter-rushing: start moving forward at match-start without waiting for sensor detection. Counter-flanking: spin faster between scans. Counter-edge-trap: maintain center position as a 'home' state.
steps.student_prompt: Choose one tactic to add to your robot and describe how you'll implement it.
steps.reflection_question: Can you design a robot that beats a RUSH bot but loses to an EDGE-TRAP bot on purpose? What does that tell you about rock-paper-scissors in robotics?

completion_badge.name: Ring Strategist
completion_badge.description: Analyzed and countered sumo tactics

challenges.title: Reactive Defense
challenges.subtitle: Sensor-triggered block programs
challenges.description: Build a reactive defense system using Blocks — when sensors detect force from a specific direction, counter-push in the opposite direction.
learning_objectives: Read motor current as a force proxy
Implement reactive counter-push
Balance offense and defense in a state machine

steps.tutor_message: When something pushes your robot, the motors fight harder — and motor current goes up. We can read that as a 'being-pushed' signal. Left motor current spike → being pushed from left → counter-push RIGHT.
steps.reflection_question: What other sensor could detect being pushed if motor current sensing isn't available? (Hint: think about acceleration or contact switches)

steps.tutor_message: Build it in Blocks: IF (left_motor_current > threshold AND right_motor_current < threshold) → boost right motor by 20% AND turn slightly left. Mirror for the other side. Add a 100ms debounce so noise doesn't trigger it.
steps.student_prompt: Build the reactive counter-push block program and tune the threshold.

steps.tutor_message: Push the robot from different sides by hand and observe the counter-response. Does it push back? Does it happen fast enough? Log the current values that trigger a response.
steps.reflection_question: What happens if you set the threshold too low? Too high?

completion_badge.name: Counter Pusher
completion_badge.description: Built a sensor-reactive defense system

challenges.title: Mass and Momentum
challenges.subtitle: Physics of the push
challenges.description: p = mv. A heavier robot at the same speed has more momentum and is harder to stop. But mass has limits in sumo. Explore the physics of collisions.
learning_objectives: Apply conservation of momentum to robot collisions
Calculate impulse and contact force
Understand why rules cap robot weight

steps.tutor_message: In a 1D elastic collision: if robot A (mass m1, velocity v1) hits stationary robot B (mass m2), B's final velocity = 2m1·v1/(m1+m2). If m1 = m2 (equal mass), B gets all the velocity and A stops — like pool balls. If A is 3× heavier, B gets 1.5×v1 but A keeps 0.5×v1.
steps.reflection_question: Competition rules cap sumo robots at 500g. Why? What would happen if one robot were 10× heavier?

steps.tutor_message: Roll two robots of different masses at each other at the same speed. Measure how far each travels after collision. Plot mass ratio vs post-collision distance ratio. Does it match the formula?
steps.student_prompt: Run 3 collisions with different mass ratios and record results.

steps.tutor_message: Real sumo robots maximize momentum within the weight limit — heavy but fast. They also lower their center of mass to resist tipping (torque = force × height). Design wins when physics is on your side.
steps.reflection_question: Given a fixed weight budget, which is more valuable: doubling mass or doubling speed? Show the math.

completion_badge.name: Collision Analyst
completion_badge.description: Applied momentum physics to robot design

challenges.title: Strategy Tree
challenges.subtitle: Build a decision-making flowchart
challenges.description: Complex sumo behavior can be built from simple if-then branches. Design a decision tree and implement it — this is the foundation of all autonomous agents.
learning_objectives: Design a multi-level decision tree
Implement conditional logic in code
Trace execution through a tree

steps.tutor_message: Draw a decision tree: root = 'line detected?'. YES → ESCAPE. NO → 'opponent detected?'. YES → ATTACK. NO → 'last seen direction?'. LEFT → rotate left. RIGHT → rotate right. UNKNOWN → SEARCH. Every sumo robot is a tree like this — just deeper.
steps.student_prompt: Draw your decision tree on paper or describe it with indentation.

steps.tutor_message: Now implement your tree in Python pseudocode, then in Blocks. Trace it on paper: pick a sensor state and follow the tree to the output. Does it always reach a leaf?
steps.student_prompt: Implement your decision tree in the Code or Blocks editor.

steps.tutor_message: A complete decision tree has a defined output for EVERY possible sensor combination. List all possible combinations (line L/R, range near/far). Does your tree cover them all?
steps.reflection_question: What happens in your tree if BOTH line sensors fire AND the range sensor detects an opponent simultaneously?

completion_badge.name: Logic Architect
completion_badge.description: Built a complete sumo decision tree

challenges.title: The Counter-Attack
challenges.subtitle: Turn your opponent's push against them
challenges.description: Aikido uses an attacker's momentum against them. A sumo robot can do the same: sidestep a rush and let the attacker fly past to the edge.
learning_objectives: Implement a timed sidestep maneuver
Understand reaction-time vs commit-time tradeoffs
Test a strategy against two different opponent types

steps.tutor_message: When a rusher charges in a straight line, it takes them 300–500ms to react to a direction change. If you sidestep 90° in 200ms, they miss. Then immediately re-engage from the side — that's their weakest spot.
steps.reflection_question: How do you know when the opponent is 'rushing' vs just approaching normally? What sensor data tells you?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: IF opponent_closing_speed > threshold → sidestep 90° → pause 100ms → drive toward opponent's expected landing zone. You're predicting where they'll end up.
steps.student_prompt: Build the sidestep counter-attack in Blocks or Python.

steps.tutor_message: Test against two robots: one that rushes, one that approaches slowly. The sidestep should work well against the rusher and leave you exposed against the slow approach. That's the tradeoff.
steps.reflection_question: What additional sensor data would let you tell a rush from a slow approach BEFORE committing to the sidestep?

completion_badge.name: Aikido Bot
completion_badge.description: Used opponent momentum as a weapon

challenges.title: Tournament Mode
challenges.subtitle: 3 bouts, best of 3
challenges.description: Run a structured 3-match tournament. Between each match, analyze what happened and adjust one parameter. Iterate toward a winning design.
learning_objectives: Apply the iterate-and-improve engineering cycle
Make data-driven parameter adjustments
Understand variance in robot performance

steps.tutor_message: Before match 1, write down your robot's current settings: attack speed, scan speed, line threshold, counter-attack delay. This is your baseline — you'll compare everything to this.
steps.student_prompt: Record your baseline settings.

steps.tutor_message: Run 3 matches. After each, write one sentence: what failed and what you'll change. Only change ONE parameter between matches so you can isolate cause and effect.
steps.student_prompt: Run 3 matches and log one change between each.

steps.tutor_message: Compare your final performance to the baseline. What improved? What made things worse unexpectedly? This process — run, measure, adjust — is engineering in its purest form.
steps.reflection_question: If you could only make one more change, what would it be and why? Back it up with data from your match log.

completion_badge.name: Iterative Engineer
completion_badge.description: Ran a 3-match improvement tournament

challenges.title: Gyro-Assisted Heading
challenges.subtitle: Hold direction under push
challenges.description: When pushed sideways, a robot with only wheel encoders drifts off its attack line. A gyroscope measures angular velocity and can correct heading in real time.
learning_objectives: Understand gyroscope integration for heading
Implement heading-lock feedback control
Distinguish between gyro drift and real turns

steps.tutor_message: A gyroscope measures angular velocity (°/s). Integrate it over time → heading angle. If heading drifts from target, increase one motor and decrease the other to correct. This is a simple P-controller on heading.
steps.reflection_question: Gyroscopes drift over time — the angle slowly creeps even when stationary. How do you calibrate out the offset?

steps.tutor_message: Implement heading-lock: set target_heading at match start. Every 10ms: error = target - current_heading. motor_correction = Kp × error. left_motor -= correction, right_motor += correction.
steps.student_prompt: Implement and tune the heading-lock P-controller. What Kp value works best?

steps.tutor_message: Test it: drive toward a target while someone pushes you sideways. Does the robot recover to the original heading? How many seconds does it take?
steps.reflection_question: What happens to your heading-lock if the gyro saturates (spins faster than max measurable rate)?

completion_badge.name: Heading Controller
completion_badge.description: Implemented gyro-based heading lock

challenges.title: Force Feedback Control
challenges.subtitle: Read motor current to sense impact
challenges.description: Motor current is proportional to torque — and torque spikes when you hit something hard. Use current sensing as a collision detector and impact force estimator.
learning_objectives: Relate motor current to mechanical force
Implement current-threshold collision detection
Use impact force data to control response

steps.tutor_message: F = k·I where I is motor current and k is the motor's torque constant. A sudden current spike (e.g., 2× normal) means a collision. The bigger the spike, the harder the hit. You can estimate opponent mass from how much the spike decays.
steps.reflection_question: What other sources could cause a current spike besides collision? (Hills, sticky mats, wheel jamming)

steps.tutor_message: Drive into a wall at three speeds (25%, 50%, 75% power). Log the current spike peak each time. Plot it — is the relationship linear?
steps.student_prompt: Log current spikes at 3 speeds and plot the result.

steps.tutor_message: Use impact magnitude to adapt strategy: small spike (light opponent) → increase push speed. Large spike (heavy opponent) → drop low and push longer with max power. Adaptive behavior from a single sensor.
steps.reflection_question: If the spike sensor triggers on a wall corner instead of an opponent, how could you add a second check to distinguish them?

completion_badge.name: Impact Analyst
completion_badge.description: Used motor current to estimate collision force

challenges.title: PID Push Control
challenges.subtitle: Tune a controller for maximum push
challenges.description: A PID controller makes a motor system track a target precisely. Use it to maintain maximum pushing force without wheel slip, even as the opponent resists.
learning_objectives: Implement a full PID controller in code
Tune Kp, Ki, Kd parameters systematically
Understand integral windup and how to prevent it

steps.tutor_message: PID: output = Kp·e + Ki·∫e·dt + Kd·de/dt, where e = target_speed - current_speed. Kp reacts to current error. Ki corrects persistent offset. Kd dampens oscillation. Use encoder feedback for current_speed.
steps.reflection_question: What happens if Ki is too large when the robot is being pushed? (Hint: the integral term keeps accumulating)

steps.tutor_message: Start with Kd=0, Ki=0. Increase Kp until the robot oscillates. Halve it — that's your starting Kp. Then slowly add Kd to reduce oscillation. Add Ki only if you see steady-state offset. Log speed vs time for each step.
steps.student_prompt: Tune Kp, Ki, Kd using the Ziegler-Nichols method described above.

steps.tutor_message: Integral windup: when the robot is stuck against a wall, the integral term keeps growing. When the wall is removed, the robot lurches. Fix: clamp the integral term to a max value.
steps.reflection_question: How do you choose the integral clamp value? Too small defeats the purpose; too large allows windup.

completion_badge.name: PID Tuner
completion_badge.description: Implemented and tuned a PID push controller

challenges.title: Vision-Based Edge Detection
challenges.subtitle: See the ring boundary with a camera
challenges.description: Replace IR line sensors with computer vision: detect the white ring boundary using a downward-facing camera and image segmentation.
learning_objectives: Apply color thresholding to detect the ring boundary
Convert image coordinates to robot-relative positions
Integrate vision-based detection into the state machine

steps.tutor_message: The ring boundary is white on black — high contrast. In HSV color space, white has high Value (V > 200) and low Saturation (S < 30). Threshold on these values to get a binary mask. The white pixels ARE the boundary.
steps.reflection_question: What would happen to your threshold if the arena is under uneven lighting?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: capture frame → convert to HSV → threshold → find contours → if white contour area in bottom 20% of image > threshold → 'line ahead'. The position of the contour tells you left/right/center approach.
steps.student_prompt: Implement the vision-based edge detector and test it against the physical ring.

steps.tutor_message: Compare vision-based detection to your original IR sensors: reaction time, false positive rate, and performance under different lighting. Vision is slower but gives positional information (left vs right vs center) that IR can't easily provide.
steps.reflection_question: Name one scenario where IR sensors would outperform camera-based detection, and one where camera wins.

completion_badge.name: Vision Guard
completion_badge.description: Replaced IR sensors with camera-based edge detection

challenges.title: Autonomous Sumo Agent
challenges.subtitle: A full AI-driven strategy
challenges.description: Combine everything: vision edge detection, PID push control, gyro heading, and an adaptive decision tree. Build a complete autonomous sumo agent.
learning_objectives: Integrate multiple subsystems into a coherent agent
Understand software architecture for autonomous robots
Debug emergent behavior in complex systems

steps.tutor_message: Your agent needs layers: PERCEPTION (read all sensors → produce state estimate), PLANNING (current state → desired action), CONTROL (desired action → motor commands). This is the classic sense-plan-act loop. Draw your architecture before coding.
steps.student_prompt: Draw your system architecture: inputs, processing layers, and outputs.

steps.tutor_message: Wire all subsystems together: vision_edge → escape_behavior, range_sensor → attack_planner, gyro → heading_lock, current_sensor → force_adapter. Each subsystem publishes its output; the planner reads them all.
steps.student_prompt: Integrate all previous challenge subsystems into one agent and test.

steps.tutor_message: When multiple systems interact, emergent behaviors appear — sometimes good, sometimes not. Log unexpected behaviors during the match. These 'bugs' are often the most interesting robotics research.
steps.reflection_question: What was the most surprising behavior your agent exhibited? Was it a bug or an accidental feature?

completion_badge.name: Autonomous Agent
completion_badge.description: Built a complete autonomous sumo agent

challenges.title: Multi-Robot Dynamics
challenges.subtitle: Two robots, one ring — the physics of chaos
challenges.description: With two robots on the same team in a ring, coordination becomes critical. Study multi-robot collision dynamics and design a cooperative strategy.
learning_objectives: Model multi-robot collision physics
Design inter-robot communication protocol
Coordinate attack patterns between two autonomous agents

steps.tutor_message: Two autonomous robots in a ring will eventually collide with each other. That's 'friendly fire' — and it can push your own robot out. Fix: assign unique IDs and have each robot broadcast its position. Never push a robot with your own team ID.
steps.reflection_question: What's the simplest possible communication between two robots that prevents friendly fire?

steps.tutor_message: A simple cooperative strategy: Robot A sweeps left, Robot B sweeps right. They push the opponent toward the center between them — then both drive in simultaneously. This 'pincer' move is hard to escape.
steps.student_prompt: Implement a pincer strategy: assign roles (A=left, B=right) and coordinate via local broadcast.

steps.tutor_message: If the opponent has TWO robots too, this becomes a 4-agent game. Teams with centralized coordination can outplan teams where each robot acts independently. This is why drones need swarm intelligence.
steps.reflection_question: In a 2v2 match, what's the best target assignment strategy: both attack the same opponent, or each picks one?

completion_badge.name: Swarm Tactician
completion_badge.description: Coordinated two robots in a shared arena

packs.name: Maze Marathon
packs.description: Navigate labyrinths from simple wall-following to full SLAM. Every path teaches a new algorithm.

challenges.title: Left-Hand Rule
challenges.subtitle: Always hug the left wall
challenges.description: The left-hand rule is the oldest maze algorithm: keep your left hand on the wall and you'll eventually find the exit — if it exists. Learn when it works and when it fails.
learning_objectives: Implement left-wall following behavior
Understand algorithm completeness and correctness
Identify maze topologies where left-hand rule fails

steps.tutor_message: The left-hand rule: IF left wall absent → turn left. ELSE IF front wall absent → go straight. ELSE IF right wall absent → turn right. ELSE → turn around (dead end). It's only three decisions — but it solves most simple mazes!
steps.reflection_question: Draw a small maze on paper and trace the left-hand rule by hand. Did it find the exit?

steps.tutor_message: Implement the rule using sensor readings: left_wall, front_wall, right_wall (boolean). Use the Rules editor: trigger = no_left_wall, action = turn_left. Add all four cases.
steps.student_prompt: Implement all four left-hand rule cases in the Rules editor or Blocks.

steps.tutor_message: The left-hand rule fails on 'island mazes' — mazes where the exit is inside a loop not connected to the outer wall. The robot just circles the loop forever. Can you design such a maze?
steps.reflection_question: What's the minimum maze structure that defeats the left-hand rule?

completion_badge.name: Wall Hugger
completion_badge.description: Implemented left-hand wall following

challenges.title: Right-Turn Only
challenges.subtitle: A navigation constraint
challenges.description: What if you can ONLY turn right? Some mazes are designed to be solved with only right turns. Explore how a constraint changes what paths are reachable.
learning_objectives: Understand how navigation constraints limit reachable paths
Compare right-turn-only vs left-hand rule coverage
Identify when constraints are advantages

steps.tutor_message: With only right turns available, you can still navigate — but some mazes become impossible. The path you take is always the rightmost option at every junction. This guarantees a specific traversal order of the maze's rooms.
steps.reflection_question: On which type of maze does right-turn-only ALWAYS find the exit — and which type does it always fail?

steps.tutor_message: Implement right-turn-only: IF right wall absent → turn right. ELSE IF front wall absent → go straight. ELSE IF left wall absent → turn left. ELSE → dead end, but NO backing up allowed — you must rotate 180° in place.
steps.student_prompt: Implement right-turn-only in the Rules editor.

steps.tutor_message: Run both algorithms (left-hand and right-turn-only) on the same maze. Map the paths they take. One might be shorter — why?
steps.reflection_question: Is there a maze where right-turn-only is faster than left-hand rule, and vice versa?

completion_badge.name: Right-Turner
completion_badge.description: Navigated with a single-turn constraint

challenges.title: Dead End Escape
challenges.subtitle: Backtrack when stuck
challenges.description: Dead ends are unavoidable in complex mazes. Learn to detect them, mark them as visited, and backtrack — this is the core of depth-first search.
learning_objectives: Detect and classify dead ends
Implement backtracking
Understand visited-state tracking

steps.tutor_message: A dead end is any cell with walls on 3 sides (only one opening: the way you came in). Detect it by reading front, left, and right sensors simultaneously. All three blocked → dead end.
steps.reflection_question: How do you know which direction is 'the way you came in' when you're inside the dead end?

steps.tutor_message: Mark dead ends in a grid: store a 2D array where each cell is 'visited' or 'dead_end'. On backtrack, don't revisit dead_end cells. This prevents the robot from re-exploring the same dead ends.
steps.student_prompt: Add a visited grid to your wall-follower and mark dead ends.

steps.tutor_message: Backtracking: store a stack of directions taken. At a dead end, pop the stack and reverse the direction. If all neighbors are marked dead, pop again. This is exactly how DFS explores a graph.
steps.reflection_question: What data structure is a 'stack'? How does it enable backtracking?

completion_badge.name: Backtracker
completion_badge.description: Implemented dead-end detection and backtracking

challenges.title: Junction Decisions
challenges.subtitle: Choosing at T and cross intersections
challenges.description: T-junctions and crossroads are decision points. What rule should govern which path to take first? Order matters for how quickly you find the exit.
learning_objectives: Classify junction types (T, cross, Y)
Implement a consistent junction decision policy
Understand how decision ordering affects traversal time

steps.tutor_message: Junctions by open directions: 2-way = hallway (no decision). 3-way (T) = one fork. 4-way (cross) = three options (you came from one side). Draw each type on paper and label which directions are available.
steps.student_prompt: Draw a T-junction and cross-junction and label all possible next moves.

steps.tutor_message: Your decision policy at junctions determines the traversal order. Three common policies: (1) Always go straight first. (2) Always take the leftmost turn. (3) Go toward unexplored cells (requires a map). Implement option 1 and 2 and compare.
steps.student_prompt: Implement a junction policy in Blocks and test it on a maze with at least 3 junctions.

steps.tutor_message: A smarter heuristic: at each junction, go toward the direction closest to the (known or estimated) goal. This is a greedy strategy — not always optimal, but usually faster than random.
steps.reflection_question: When does the greedy junction heuristic fail? Design a maze where it leads to the longest possible path.

completion_badge.name: Junction Master
completion_badge.description: Implemented and compared junction decision policies

challenges.title: Speed Run
challenges.subtitle: Solve the maze as fast as possible
challenges.description: First solve the maze slowly to map it. Then run it at full speed using the known map. This two-phase approach is how competition maze robots work.
learning_objectives: Separate exploration phase from exploitation phase
Optimize motor speed for a known path
Understand the explore-exploit tradeoff

steps.tutor_message: Phase 1 (exploration): run the maze slowly, logging every turn at every junction as a string like 'LRSLLS' (Left, Right, Straight). When you reach the exit, you have the complete path.
steps.student_prompt: Run the maze and record the turn string.

steps.tutor_message: Simplify the path string: LBL = S (left-back-left = straight), RBR = S. These cancel out: you went into a dead end and came back. Remove all unnecessary detours, leaving the shortest path string.
steps.student_prompt: Simplify your turn string using cancellation rules.

steps.tutor_message: Phase 2 (exploitation): run the optimized path at maximum speed using only the stored turn string — no sensor scanning needed. Just count junctions and execute the next turn.
steps.reflection_question: What can go wrong during the speed run that didn't happen during the slow exploration?

completion_badge.name: Speed Solver
completion_badge.description: Explored then exploited the optimal path

challenges.title: Draw the Map
challenges.subtitle: Sketch the maze as you navigate
challenges.description: SketchBot's pen can draw the maze map in real time as it explores. Each cell visited gets drawn. The result is a physical record of the exploration.
learning_objectives: Maintain a 2D occupancy grid from sensor data
Visualize exploration state as a drawing
Understand dead reckoning with wheel encoders

steps.tutor_message: Maintain a 2D grid where each cell represents one maze corridor. As you drive, track your position using wheel encoders (dead reckoning). When you sense a wall, mark that cell's wall. When you visit a cell, mark it 'visited'.
steps.reflection_question: Dead reckoning accumulates error — small wheel slip causes position drift. After 10 cells, how far off might your position estimate be?

steps.tutor_message: After exploring the maze, send the grid to SketchBot and let it draw the map: explored cells as light squares, walls as thick lines, the path taken as a dashed line.

payload.prompt: a hand-drawn maze map showing explored cells as open squares with light lines, unvisited cells as filled dark squares, walls as thick borders, and the exploration path as a dashed line through visited cells

steps.tutor_message: Compare the drawn map to the actual maze. Where did dead reckoning drift? Are the walls in the right places? How could you use AprilTags to correct position drift?
steps.reflection_question: What sensor could give absolute position (not relative) and eliminate dead reckoning drift entirely?

completion_badge.name: Cartographer
completion_badge.description: Drew a real-time maze map from sensor data

challenges.title: Depth-First Search
challenges.subtitle: Explore every branch before backtracking
challenges.description: DFS is the foundational graph search algorithm. It follows one path as deep as possible before backtracking. Implement it for maze navigation.
learning_objectives: Implement DFS using a stack
Understand time and space complexity of DFS
Apply graph theory to physical maze navigation

steps.tutor_message: DFS pseudocode: push(start). WHILE stack not empty: cell = pop(). IF cell == goal → done. FOR each unvisited neighbor: mark visited, push neighbor. The stack is the robot's 'memory' of where to backtrack to.
steps.reflection_question: What determines the ORDER that DFS visits cells? How does the order neighbors are pushed affect the path?

steps.tutor_message: Implement DFS in Python for the maze: represent the maze as a dictionary {cell: [neighbors]}. Run DFS and print the path from start to goal. Then simulate it on the physical robot.
steps.student_prompt: Code DFS in Python on the given maze representation and run it.

steps.tutor_message: DFS time complexity: O(V + E) — visits every vertex and edge once. Space: O(V) for the visited set + O(depth) for the stack. In the worst case (linear maze), the stack depth equals the entire maze length.
steps.reflection_question: Does DFS guarantee the shortest path? If not, what algorithm does?

completion_badge.name: Deep Searcher
completion_badge.description: Implemented depth-first search for maze navigation

challenges.title: Breadth-First Search
challenges.subtitle: Find the shortest path guaranteed
challenges.description: BFS explores level by level — all cells one step away first, then two steps, and so on. This guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
learning_objectives: Implement BFS using a queue
Prove BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
Compare BFS vs DFS path quality

steps.tutor_message: BFS uses a QUEUE (FIFO) instead of a stack. enqueue(start). WHILE queue not empty: cell = dequeue(). IF cell == goal → done. FOR each unvisited neighbor: mark visited, track parent, enqueue. Trace back parents from goal to start to get the path.
steps.reflection_question: Why does FIFO ordering guarantee BFS explores by distance? What would happen if you used a random-access structure instead?

steps.tutor_message: Implement BFS in Python on the same maze as DFS. Track parent pointers so you can reconstruct the path. Compare the path length from both algorithms.
steps.student_prompt: Code BFS with parent tracking and extract the shortest path.

steps.tutor_message: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted mazes. But in a physical robot, 'shortest' by cell count isn't always fastest — a path with fewer turns is faster to execute. This is why A* uses a cost function that includes turn penalties.
steps.reflection_question: Design a maze where the BFS shortest path (fewest cells) takes longer to drive than a slightly longer path (more cells, fewer turns).

completion_badge.name: Shortest Pather
completion_badge.description: Implemented BFS to guarantee the shortest maze path

challenges.title: A* Pathfinding
challenges.subtitle: Heuristic-guided shortest path
challenges.description: A* combines BFS's guarantee of shortest path with a heuristic that guides it toward the goal — exploring smarter, not harder.
learning_objectives: Understand the f = g + h formula
Choose and evaluate admissible heuristics
Implement A* with a priority queue

steps.tutor_message: A* scores each cell: f = g + h. g = cost from start (known). h = estimated cost to goal (heuristic). For a grid maze, a good h is Manhattan distance: |x_goal - x| + |y_goal - y|. An admissible heuristic never overestimates — this guarantees optimality.
steps.reflection_question: Is Euclidean distance an admissible heuristic for a grid maze? Why or why not?

steps.tutor_message: Implement A* with a priority queue (heapq in Python). Always expand the cell with the lowest f score. Track g and parent for each cell. The path to goal is found by tracing parents.
steps.student_prompt: Implement A* in Python with Manhattan distance heuristic.

steps.tutor_message: On a 10×10 maze, compare cells explored: DFS (many), BFS (many), A* (fewer). A* typically explores far fewer cells because the heuristic prunes the wrong directions early.
steps.reflection_question: What happens to A* performance when the heuristic is h=0 (always)? What algorithm does it become?

completion_badge.name: Pathfinder
completion_badge.description: Implemented A* with an admissible heuristic

challenges.title: Dynamic Maze
challenges.subtitle: Walls that appear and disappear
challenges.description: A static maze is solved once. A dynamic maze — where walls can change while you navigate — requires replanning. This is the core of robotics in the real world.
learning_objectives: Understand the need for replanning in dynamic environments
Implement a simple replanning trigger
Compare reactive vs deliberative responses to change

steps.tutor_message: Real environments change. A human walks in front of the robot. A door closes. A new obstacle appears. Your static A* plan is now wrong. The simplest fix: if the sensor detects something unexpected → replan from current position.
steps.reflection_question: How often should you replan? After every step? Only on obstacle detection? What's the tradeoff?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: run A* to get plan. Execute one step. Check sensors for new obstacles. If obstacle found: update map, re-run A* from current position. Repeat. This is called 'Lifelong Planning A*' (LPA*).
steps.student_prompt: Implement replanning A* and test it by physically placing a new obstacle mid-run.

steps.tutor_message: Replanning takes time — during which the robot must stop or keep moving on the old plan. If replanning takes 200ms and the robot moves at 30cm/s, the robot travels 6cm on a potentially wrong plan. This latency trade-off matters.
steps.reflection_question: Name two techniques to make replanning fast enough to avoid stopping.

completion_badge.name: Dynamic Navigator
completion_badge.description: Implemented replanning in a changing maze

challenges.title: Maze Generator
challenges.subtitle: Code a maze and draw it
challenges.description: Generate a random solvable maze using Recursive Backtracking and have SketchBot draw it on paper. Then solve what you just generated.
learning_objectives: Implement recursive backtracking maze generation
Understand that all mazes generated this way are perfect (no loops)
Connect generation to solution algorithms

steps.tutor_message: Recursive Backtracking: start at a random cell. Mark it visited. Pick a random unvisited neighbor → knock down the wall between them → recurse. When stuck, backtrack. Result: a perfect maze (exactly one path between any two cells).
steps.student_prompt: Implement recursive backtracking maze generation in Python for a 10×10 grid.

steps.tutor_message: Export your generated maze as wall coordinates and have SketchBot draw it — a real physical maze, generated by your own code!

payload.prompt: a 10x10 cell maze with thick wall lines, clear corridors, a marked START in the top-left cell and FINISH in the bottom-right cell, clean grid-aligned lines

steps.tutor_message: Now run BFS or A* on the generated maze structure and plot the solution path on the drawing. You built the puzzle AND the solver.
steps.student_prompt: Run your pathfinding algorithm on the generated maze and overlay the solution path.
steps.reflection_question: Recursive backtracking always generates a perfect maze. What algorithm generates mazes with loops (multiple paths between cells)?

completion_badge.name: Maze Architect
completion_badge.description: Generated and solved a maze from code

challenges.title: SLAM Basics
challenges.subtitle: Map and locate at the same time
challenges.description: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping: build a map while knowing where you are in it. The challenge is that both are unknowns — you need to solve them together.
learning_objectives: Understand the SLAM problem formulation
Implement a simple EKF-based 2D SLAM
Identify landmark-based vs feature-based SLAM

steps.tutor_message: The SLAM paradox: to build a map you need to know where you are. To know where you are, you need a map. Extended Kalman Filter SLAM solves this by maintaining a joint probability distribution over both robot pose and landmark positions — updating both simultaneously.
steps.reflection_question: If you had perfect odometry (no wheel slip, no drift), would you still need SLAM? What would make odometry 'perfect'?

steps.tutor_message: In landmark-based SLAM, you identify repeatable features (corners, AprilTags) and track them. Each landmark sighting: update the robot's position estimate AND the landmark's position estimate. Each improves the other.
steps.student_prompt: Place 4 AprilTags in the maze. Implement landmark detection and build a 2D landmark map.

steps.tutor_message: Loop closure: when the robot returns to a known location, it can correct accumulated drift in all previous estimates. This is the 'ah-ha!' moment of SLAM — suddenly the whole map snaps into better alignment.
steps.reflection_question: Without loop closure, does SLAM still work? What happens to map quality over long distances?

completion_badge.name: SLAM Pioneer
completion_badge.description: Implemented basic landmark SLAM

challenges.title: Occupancy Grid
challenges.subtitle: A probabilistic map of the maze
challenges.description: An occupancy grid stores the probability that each cell is occupied. Sensor readings update the probabilities using Bayes' theorem — the robot is always 'uncertain' about the world.
learning_objectives: Implement a Bayesian occupancy grid update
Understand log-odds representation for numerical stability
Visualize uncertainty in a sensor map

steps.tutor_message: Bayes rule for occupancy: P(occ | z) = P(z | occ) × P(occ) / P(z). We use log-odds: L = log(p/(1-p)). When sensor sees obstacle: L += log_odds_occ. When sensor sees free: L += log_odds_free. This is numerically stable and fast.
steps.reflection_question: Why use log-odds instead of just accumulating probabilities directly?

steps.tutor_message: Implement a 20×20 occupancy grid. For each ultrasonic reading: mark cells along the beam as 'free' (reduce log-odds), mark the cell at the detected range as 'occupied' (increase log-odds). Visualize the grid.
steps.student_prompt: Implement and visualize the occupancy grid from ultrasonic sweeps.

steps.tutor_message: Notice cells that never get a clear reading — they stay near 0.5 probability (maximum uncertainty). These are the robot's 'blind spots'. Plan exploration to reduce uncertainty in those regions first.
steps.reflection_question: What value of log-odds corresponds to P(occupied) = 0.5? What does that mean for that cell?

completion_badge.name: Probabilistic Mapper
completion_badge.description: Built a Bayesian occupancy grid

challenges.title: RRT Path Planning
challenges.subtitle: Rapidly-exploring random trees
challenges.description: RRT grows a tree of reachable configurations by sampling random points in space — it works in continuous state spaces where grid-based methods can't.
learning_objectives: Implement the RRT algorithm
Understand why RRT is better than grid search for continuous spaces
Visualize the tree growth and extracted path

steps.tutor_message: RRT: start with root at start position. LOOP: sample random point q_rand. Find nearest node in tree. Extend toward q_rand by step_size. If no collision, add to tree. UNTIL q_new is close to goal. The tree 'rapidly explores' the space.
steps.reflection_question: Why is RRT well-suited for high-dimensional spaces (like robot arm joint angles) where grid search fails?

steps.tutor_message: Implement RRT in Python on a 2D map with obstacle rectangles. Visualize the tree growth and final path. Compare path quality to A* on the same map.
steps.student_prompt: Implement RRT and visualize the tree and extracted path.

steps.tutor_message: RRT* improves RRT by 'rewiring' the tree: when adding a new node, check if nearby nodes would have a shorter path through the new node. Over time, RRT* converges to the optimal path. RRT never does.
steps.reflection_question: What's the tradeoff between RRT (fast, suboptimal) and RRT* (slower, optimal)?

completion_badge.name: Random Planner
completion_badge.description: Implemented RRT path planning

challenges.title: Visual Odometry
challenges.subtitle: Track position from camera images
challenges.description: Visual odometry estimates how far the robot has moved by comparing consecutive camera frames. Features tracked across frames reveal the camera's motion — no wheel encoders needed.
learning_objectives: Implement feature tracking with optical flow
Estimate camera translation from feature displacement
Understand scale ambiguity in monocular VO

steps.tutor_message: Visual odometry steps: (1) Detect keypoints (corners, edges) in frame 1. (2) Track those keypoints in frame 2 using optical flow. (3) The average displacement of all tracked points = camera motion. More points = more robust estimate.
steps.reflection_question: What happens to VO accuracy when the robot moves through a featureless white wall? How do you recover?

steps.tutor_message: Implement monocular VO: use OpenCV's goodFeaturesToTrack + calcOpticalFlowPyrLK. Track features between consecutive frames and estimate translation from median feature displacement vector.
steps.student_prompt: Implement monocular visual odometry on camera frames from the maze navigation.

steps.tutor_message: Monocular VO has a scale ambiguity problem: you can estimate direction and relative motion but not absolute scale. A robot that's 10cm tall looks identical to one that's 1m tall from the camera's math. Fix: use known object size (AprilTag) as a scale reference.
steps.reflection_question: How does stereo visual odometry solve the scale ambiguity problem? What's the minimum baseline between cameras?

completion_badge.name: Vision Odometrist
completion_badge.description: Estimated robot position from camera frames

challenges.title: Full Autonomy
challenges.subtitle: Solve an unknown maze with no human input
challenges.description: Given an unseen maze, navigate from start to finish using SLAM for mapping, A* for planning, and visual odometry for localization. Zero human intervention.
learning_objectives: Integrate SLAM, planning, and control into one pipeline
Handle unexpected situations autonomously
Evaluate autonomy by a formal metric (completion rate, time, accuracy)

steps.tutor_message: Your full autonomy pipeline: VO provides pose updates → occupancy grid builds the map → A* plans from current pose to goal → PID controller executes the plan → loop. If plan fails: replan. If localization fails: stop and recover.
steps.student_prompt: Draw and describe your complete system pipeline.

steps.tutor_message: Run 3 trials on different maze configurations you haven't seen. Log: completion rate, time to finish, distance traveled vs optimal. These metrics evaluate your system's autonomy level.
steps.student_prompt: Run 3 blind trials and log all metrics.

steps.tutor_message: Full autonomy rarely works perfectly on the first try. Identify the most common failure mode and design a targeted fix. Autonomous systems improve through structured failure analysis.
steps.reflection_question: What SAE autonomy level (0–5) does your system achieve? What would it take to get to the next level?

completion_badge.name: Full Autonomy
completion_badge.description: Solved an unknown maze with zero human intervention

challenges.title: Two Robots, One Maze
challenges.subtitle: Cooperative multi-robot exploration
challenges.description: Two robots can map a maze twice as fast if they coordinate — each explores the areas the other hasn't visited. Design a cooperative frontier-based exploration strategy.
learning_objectives: Implement frontier-based exploration
Coordinate exploration between two agents without conflict
Merge two partial maps into one consistent map

steps.tutor_message: A 'frontier' is the boundary between explored free space and unknown space. Frontier-based exploration: identify all frontiers, assign one to each robot, move there to explore it. When no frontiers remain — the map is complete.
steps.reflection_question: How do you prevent both robots from going to the same frontier simultaneously?

steps.tutor_message: Robot A broadcasts its claimed frontier. Robot B reads the claimed set and picks the closest UNCLAIMED frontier. Each robot marks its frontier as claimed before moving toward it. When finished, it un-claims and picks a new one.
steps.student_prompt: Implement frontier claiming via local broadcast and run a coordinated exploration.

steps.tutor_message: Merge partial maps: use known landmark (AprilTag at maze entrance) as shared origin. Transform Robot B's map into Robot A's coordinate frame and average overlapping cells. The merged map should be more accurate than either individual map.
steps.reflection_question: What happens to the merged map if the two robots have different position drift errors?

completion_badge.name: Swarm Explorer
completion_badge.description: Coordinated two robots for cooperative maze mapping

packs.name: Cone Ring Gauntlet
packs.description: Hunt, dodge, and sort cones inside a ring arena. Combines robotics, computer vision, and competitive speed.

challenges.title: Spot the Cone
challenges.subtitle: Identify a cone in the camera view
challenges.description: Before the robot can interact with cones, it must see them. Learn how cameras detect objects by color, shape, and size.
learning_objectives: Understand color-based object detection
Identify a cone using HSV thresholding
Find the cone's center in the image

steps.tutor_message: Traffic cones are bright orange — that's intentional! In HSV color space, orange is: H=5–20°, S=180–255, V=150–255. Anything in that range is probably a cone. Filter the camera image for those values and see what lights up.
steps.reflection_question: Why do we use HSV instead of RGB for color detection? What happens to the RGB values of orange under dim vs bright lighting?

steps.tutor_message: Apply the HSV mask and find connected regions. The largest connected orange region is probably the cone. Compute its centroid — that's where the cone is in image coordinates.

payload.prompt: a camera view diagram showing an orange cone with an HSV color mask overlay highlighting the cone region, centroid marked with a crosshair, bounding box drawn

steps.tutor_message: Orange sunlight, warning labels, and clothing can all trigger false detections. Apply a minimum area filter: ignore any orange region smaller than 500 pixels². Large blobs are cones; small blobs are noise.
steps.reflection_question: If two cones are visible, how would you detect BOTH and determine which is closer?

completion_badge.name: Cone Spotter
completion_badge.description: Detected a cone using color segmentation

challenges.title: Drive Around
challenges.subtitle: Navigate around a single cone
challenges.description: Detecting a cone is step one. Step two: don't hit it. Program a simple avoidance maneuver using the cone's image position to steer around it.
learning_objectives: Convert image coordinates to steering commands
Implement a simple obstacle avoidance behavior
Tune avoidance turning radius

steps.tutor_message: When the cone centroid is in the LEFT half of the image, the cone is to your left — turn right. If it's in the RIGHT half, turn left. If it's centered, it's straight ahead — turn more sharply to pick a side.
steps.reflection_question: What happens when the cone is at the very top of the image (far away) vs the bottom (very close)?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: steering_angle = Kp × (image_center_x - cone_x). Positive angle → turn right. Drive forward while steering. When cone leaves the camera frame, straighten out.
steps.student_prompt: Implement the steering law and test avoidance on a single cone.

steps.tutor_message: Too high Kp → robot swerves wildly. Too low → robot drives into the cone before turning enough. Find the Kp that gives a smooth arc around the cone.
