steps.reflection_question: How wide should the avoidance arc be? What determines the minimum safe passing distance?

completion_badge.name: Cone Dodger
completion_badge.description: Navigated around a single cone autonomously

challenges.title: Slalom Intro
challenges.subtitle: Weave through 3 cones
challenges.description: A slalom is the classic speed-accuracy test. 3 cones in a line — alternate left and right of each one. First without a time limit, then as fast as you can.
learning_objectives: Sequence multi-cone avoidance
Coordinate side-of-cone alternation
Balance speed and accuracy

steps.tutor_message: In a slalom you alternate sides: go right of cone 1, left of cone 2, right of cone 3. The tricky part: after passing cone 1, you need to start turning toward cone 2 early — not just avoid, but aim ahead.
steps.reflection_question: How do you know you've successfully 'passed' a cone and should switch to tracking the next one?

steps.tutor_message: Implement cone sequencing: maintain a counter (current_cone). After passing current cone (it leaves view behind), increment counter. Use a side flag (left/right) that alternates with each increment.
steps.student_prompt: Implement slalom sequencing and run the 3-cone course.

steps.tutor_message: Run the slalom 3 times, recording your time and any cones knocked over. A penalty of 5 seconds per cone touched is standard. Plot speed vs accuracy — find your optimal operating point.
steps.reflection_question: At what speed does accuracy start to degrade? Can you predict this from the geometry of the cone spacing?

completion_badge.name: Slalom Starter
completion_badge.description: Completed a 3-cone slalom course

challenges.title: Ring Gate
challenges.subtitle: Drive precisely through a ring
challenges.description: A ring gate requires precision — not just avoidance, but centering. The robot must drive exactly through the middle of a hoop placed on the ground.
learning_objectives: Detect a ring using ellipse fitting
Center the robot relative to the ring's detected position
Implement approach and pass-through control

steps.tutor_message: A ring on the ground appears as an ellipse in the camera image due to perspective. Use edge detection + Hough ellipse fitting to find it. The ellipse center is the gate center; the axes tell you its orientation and distance.
steps.reflection_question: When the ring is directly ahead (perpendicular to the camera), what shape does it appear in the image?

steps.tutor_message: Control law: lateral error = ring_center_x - image_center_x. Steer to reduce this error (like the cone law). As you approach, the ellipse grows in the image — use its size to estimate distance.
steps.student_prompt: Implement ring gate detection and centering. Test on a physical ring.

steps.tutor_message: When the ring fills > 60% of the image height, drive straight through at reduced speed. Slowing down near the gate dramatically improves pass-through accuracy.
steps.reflection_question: How would you handle a ring that's tilted 30° — not flat on the ground?

completion_badge.name: Ring Threader
completion_badge.description: Navigated precisely through a ring gate

challenges.title: Cone Course
challenges.subtitle: Complete a 5-cone obstacle course
challenges.description: A 5-cone course with a ring gate combines all previous skills. Slalom 4 cones, pass through the ring gate, reach the finish line.
learning_objectives: Sequence multiple navigation tasks into one program
Detect and classify multiple object types
Handle state transitions between task segments

steps.tutor_message: Set up the course: 4 cones in slalom formation, 1 ring gate at the end. The robot must: slalom the cones, switch to ring-detection mode, thread the gate, then go straight to finish.
steps.student_prompt: Set up the physical course and describe your plan for transitioning between task segments.

steps.tutor_message: Run the course. Your state machine should: SLALOM (count cones, alternate sides) → when ring detected → GATE (center on ring, pass through) → FINISH (drive straight). Log any failures and which state they occurred in.
steps.student_prompt: Run 3 complete course attempts and log success/failure per segment.

steps.tutor_message: Most failures happen at state transitions — the moment the robot switches from slalom to gate detection. The brief window where it's searching for the ring is when it's most likely to veer off course.
steps.reflection_question: How can you make the state transition smoother so there's no 'dead zone' between task segments?

completion_badge.name: Course Completer
completion_badge.description: Completed the 5-cone obstacle course

challenges.title: Time Trial
challenges.subtitle: Fastest clean run wins
challenges.description: Optimize your 5-cone course program for speed. This isn't about changing the hardware — it's about finding the fastest PARAMETERS that still complete the course cleanly.
learning_objectives: Systematic parameter optimization
Understand the speed-accuracy Pareto frontier
Use timed runs as a feedback signal for tuning

steps.tutor_message: Record your current best time and success rate. Write down all tunable parameters: slalom Kp, approach speed, gate threshold, transition speed. These are your optimization levers.
steps.student_prompt: Document baseline time, success rate, and all tunable parameters.

steps.tutor_message: Parameter sweep: change one parameter at a time, run 3 trials each, record mean time and DNF rate. Plot time vs parameter value. Find the sweet spot before accuracy degrades.
steps.student_prompt: Sweep at least 2 parameters and plot the results.

steps.tutor_message: Combine your best parameter settings. Run a final 5-trial comparison: baseline vs optimized. Calculate mean, standard deviation, and improvement percentage.
steps.reflection_question: Did your optimization improve mean time, consistency, or both? Which matters more in a competition?

completion_badge.name: Optimizer
completion_badge.description: Systematically improved course time through parameter tuning

challenges.title: Color-Coded Cones
challenges.subtitle: Navigate differently based on cone color
challenges.description: Red and blue cones mean different things: red = go right of this cone, blue = go left. The robot must read the color and execute the correct maneuver.
learning_objectives: Detect multiple distinct colors simultaneously
Map color to action in a lookup table
Handle ambiguous colors under different lighting

steps.tutor_message: Extend your color detector: add blue (H=100–130°) alongside orange and red. Classify each detected blob by dominant hue. Build a color → action lookup: RED = pass_right, BLUE = pass_left, ORANGE = free choice.
steps.reflection_question: Under warm (orange-tinted) lighting, how might your red vs orange classifier be confused?

steps.tutor_message: Add cone color classification to your detection pipeline. Test with individual cones of each color. Log the detection results under different lighting conditions.
steps.student_prompt: Implement multi-color detection and test it under at least 2 lighting conditions.

steps.tutor_message: Set up a 6-cone course with alternating red/blue cones in non-standard order (e.g. red, red, blue, red, blue, blue). The robot must read and respond correctly — not just alternate sides.
steps.student_prompt: Run the color-coded course and log success rate per cone.
steps.reflection_question: What minimum area (pixels²) is needed to reliably classify cone color vs just detecting it?

completion_badge.name: Color Classifier
completion_badge.description: Navigated a course using cone color commands

challenges.title: Dynamic Obstacles
challenges.subtitle: Cones that move
challenges.description: Real environments have moving objects — people, vehicles, other robots. Test your avoidance algorithm against cones that are slowly moved by hand during the run.
learning_objectives: Track moving objects using velocity estimation
Predict object position ahead of reaction time
Distinguish stopped from moving obstacles

steps.tutor_message: To track a moving cone, estimate its velocity: position_t - position_(t-1) / delta_t. Predict where it will be in 200ms: predicted = position_t + velocity × 0.2. Steer to avoid the PREDICTED position, not the current one.
steps.reflection_question: What's the minimum number of frames needed to estimate a cone's velocity? What's the tradeoff with using more frames?

steps.tutor_message: Implement position tracking + linear velocity prediction. For cones being pushed slowly (< 10cm/s), how much does prediction help vs just avoiding the current position?
steps.student_prompt: Implement cone velocity estimation and predictive avoidance.

steps.tutor_message: Run the course while someone slowly pushes two cones across the path. Record: how many collisions without prediction vs with prediction. Quantify the improvement.
steps.reflection_question: When does your linear prediction model fail? What kind of motion would it handle poorly?

completion_badge.name: Dynamic Dodger
completion_badge.description: Avoided moving obstacles using predictive tracking

challenges.title: Precision Parking
challenges.subtitle: Park between cone markers
challenges.description: Parking between cones tests lateral precision. The robot must stop exactly centered between two marker cones — within 2cm — without touching them.
learning_objectives: Implement a position-accuracy control loop
Use visual feedback for fine lateral correction
Understand the stopping accuracy vs approach speed tradeoff

steps.tutor_message: Two cones define a parking space. The robot must center between them. In the camera image: left_cone_x and right_cone_x → target_x = (left_x + right_x)/2. Steer to minimize |camera_center_x - target_x|.
steps.reflection_question: How does the robot know when it's 'inside' the parking space and should stop, vs still approaching it from outside?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: detect both cones simultaneously, compute target centerline, approach at low speed while correcting lateral error. Stop when the robot length is fully inside the cone markers (estimated from depth).
steps.student_prompt: Implement precision parking and test for ±2cm centering accuracy.

steps.tutor_message: Measure actual parking accuracy with a ruler: distance from each cone after parking. Run 10 trials and report mean and standard deviation of lateral error.
steps.reflection_question: What's causing the residual error? Sensor noise, control lag, or wheel slip during stopping?

completion_badge.name: Precision Parker
completion_badge.description: Parked within 2cm of center between cone markers

challenges.title: Ring Sort
challenges.subtitle: Classify rings by size
challenges.description: Three rings of different sizes are placed around the course. The robot must detect each ring, estimate its real-world diameter from the image, and navigate them in order: smallest to largest.
learning_objectives: Estimate real-world size from apparent image size and distance
Sort detected objects by a derived property
Sequence tasks based on classification results

steps.tutor_message: Apparent size (pixels) and real size (cm) relate through distance: real_size = apparent_size_px × distance / focal_length_px. If you know the focal length and can estimate distance (from the ellipse shape), you can compute real diameter.
steps.reflection_question: Two rings of the same size at different distances appear different sizes in the image. How do you tell them apart?

steps.tutor_message: Do a scanning pass: drive slowly around the arena, detect all three rings, estimate their diameters. Sort them: small < medium < large. Then plan the order of navigation: smallest first.
steps.student_prompt: Implement ring detection, size estimation, and sorting. Verify with physical measurements.

steps.tutor_message: Execute the sorted sequence: drive to smallest ring, pass through, drive to medium, pass through, drive to largest. All three must be passed in order without re-scanning.
steps.reflection_question: What happens if two rings have nearly identical diameters? How much size difference is needed for reliable classification?

completion_badge.name: Size Sorter
completion_badge.description: Classified and navigated rings by estimated real-world size

challenges.title: Full Gauntlet
challenges.subtitle: Color cones, moving obstacles, ring gates — all at once
challenges.description: The complete gauntlet: 4 color-coded cones, 2 moving obstacles, 3 ring gates of different sizes. Complete all tasks in the correct order with no human input.
learning_objectives: Integrate multiple detection and navigation behaviors
Handle unexpected task sequencing
Evaluate system robustness under combined challenge conditions

steps.tutor_message: Map out the gauntlet: identify all task segments and their order. Draw a state machine with transitions. Identify which sensor configurations trigger each transition.
steps.student_prompt: Draw the complete state machine for the full gauntlet.

steps.tutor_message: Run the full gauntlet 3 times. Score: +10 per cone passed correctly, +15 per ring gate threaded, -10 per collision. Log your score and the failure point for each attempt.
steps.student_prompt: Run 3 attempts and log score + failure point each time.

steps.tutor_message: The most common failure: wrong state transition at the wrong time. Review your logs: which state triggered unexpectedly? What sensor reading caused it? Fix the threshold and re-run.
steps.reflection_question: What's the biggest bottleneck in your system — perception, planning, or control? How would you test each in isolation?

completion_badge.name: Gauntlet Champion
completion_badge.description: Completed the full multi-challenge gauntlet

challenges.title: Deep Cone Detector
challenges.subtitle: Train a neural detector for cones
challenges.description: Color thresholding is fragile. A trained neural network handles lighting variation, partial occlusion, and novel cone colors. Train a lightweight cone detector.
learning_objectives: Understand object detection vs classification
Collect and label a small training dataset
Train and evaluate a MobileNet SSD-style detector

steps.tutor_message: Collect 200 images of cones under varied conditions: different angles, distances, lighting, backgrounds. Label each with bounding boxes. This is the hardest part of ML — clean data wins over fancy models.
steps.student_prompt: Collect and label 200 cone images using a labeling tool.

steps.tutor_message: Fine-tune a MobileNetV2 backbone on your dataset using transfer learning. Use 80% for training, 20% for validation. Track mAP (mean Average Precision) — aim for >0.85 on validation.
steps.student_prompt: Train the detector and report validation mAP.

steps.tutor_message: Export to TFLite or ONNX for real-time inference on the robot. Measure inference speed (ms per frame). Compare detection quality vs your old HSV threshold method under 3 different lighting conditions.
steps.reflection_question: When does the neural detector FAIL that the HSV threshold succeeds? When does HSV fail that neural succeeds?

completion_badge.name: Neural Detector
completion_badge.description: Trained a neural cone detector from real images

challenges.title: Monocular Depth
challenges.subtitle: Estimate cone distance from a single camera
challenges.description: A single camera can't directly measure distance — but apparent size, position, and learned priors can give useful estimates. Implement monocular depth estimation for cones.
learning_objectives: Apply the pinhole camera model for depth estimation
Use known object size as a depth reference
Evaluate accuracy vs a ground-truth distance sensor

steps.tutor_message: Pinhole camera model: object_height_px = (real_height × focal_length) / distance. Rearranged: distance = (real_height × focal_length) / object_height_px. If a cone is 30cm tall and appears 120px tall in a camera with 400px focal length: distance = (30 × 400)/120 = 100cm.
steps.student_prompt: Calibrate your camera's focal length using the formula and a cone at a known distance.

steps.tutor_message: Implement per-cone distance estimation: detect cone bounding box height, apply the formula. Test at 30, 60, 90, 120, 150cm ground truth distances. Plot estimated vs actual.
steps.student_prompt: Implement and evaluate monocular depth at 5 distances.

steps.tutor_message: Error increases with distance because small pixel changes correspond to large real-world changes. Near (30cm): maybe ±2cm error. Far (150cm): maybe ±15cm. This non-linear error is inherent to the pinhole model.
steps.reflection_question: How would lens distortion (barrel/pincushion) affect these depth estimates, and how do you correct for it?

completion_badge.name: Depth Estimator
completion_badge.description: Estimated cone distance from monocular camera

challenges.title: Optimal Path Through Cones
challenges.subtitle: Traveling Salesman for the gauntlet
challenges.description: With all cone and ring positions known, find the shortest path through them all. This is related to the Traveling Salesman Problem — NP-hard in general, but solvable for small N.
learning_objectives: Formulate the course as an optimization problem
Implement a greedy nearest-neighbor heuristic
Compare greedy vs brute-force for small N

steps.tutor_message: The Traveling Salesman Problem: given N waypoints, find the shortest route that visits all of them exactly once. For the gauntlet: N = 4 cones + 3 rings = 7 waypoints. Brute force is 7! = 5040 routes. Fast enough to check all!
steps.reflection_question: At what N does brute force become impractical? What's your laptop's approximate limit?

steps.tutor_message: Implement brute-force TSP for N≤10: generate all permutations of waypoints, compute total path length for each, return the minimum. For N=7, this runs in milliseconds.
steps.student_prompt: Implement brute-force TSP and find the optimal gauntlet order.

steps.tutor_message: Execute the optimal order vs your original hand-designed order. Measure the distance difference. How much shorter is the optimal route?
steps.reflection_question: The gauntlet has ordering constraints (must pass red before blue). How do you add constraints to the TSP formulation?

completion_badge.name: Route Optimizer
completion_badge.description: Found and executed the optimal path through the gauntlet

challenges.title: Ring Pose Estimation
challenges.subtitle: Find the ring's 3D orientation
challenges.description: A tilted ring requires knowing its full 3D pose — not just where it is in the image, but which direction its axis points. Implement PnP-based ring pose estimation.
learning_objectives: Understand the PnP (Perspective-n-Point) problem
Use solvePnP to recover 3D pose from 2D correspondences
Decompose the rotation matrix into Euler angles

steps.tutor_message: PnP: given N 3D world points and their corresponding 2D image projections, find the camera-to-world transformation (rotation R and translation t). For a ring, 8 points on its perimeter give a robust estimate.
steps.reflection_question: Why do you need at least 4 point correspondences for PnP? What degeneracy occurs with only 3?

steps.tutor_message: Sample 8 equidistant points on the ring's detected ellipse. Map them to 3D world coordinates (assuming known ring radius). Call cv2.solvePnP. Extract rotation vector rvec → Rodrigues → rotation matrix → Euler angles.
steps.student_prompt: Implement ring PnP pose estimation and verify roll/pitch/yaw against a protractor.

steps.tutor_message: Use the ring's 3D pose to plan the approach trajectory: align the robot's direction with the ring's axis, not just the ring's center. A tilted ring requires approaching from a specific angle to pass through cleanly.
steps.reflection_question: At what tilt angle does it become impossible to pass through a ring without the robot frame catching the edge?

completion_badge.name: Pose Estimator
completion_badge.description: Estimated full 3D ring pose using PnP

challenges.title: Autonomous Gauntlet
challenges.subtitle: Zero human input, all 10 elements
challenges.description: The complete autonomous gauntlet: the robot is placed in a start position and must detect, plan, and complete all course elements without any human intervention.
learning_objectives: Operate a full perception-planning-control pipeline autonomously
Handle task failure and recovery without human help
Evaluate autonomy under real-world variation

steps.tutor_message: Your autonomous pipeline: SCAN (sweep for all objects, build position map) → PLAN (TSP on waypoints) → EXECUTE (navigate each waypoint, handle failures) → RECOVER (if stuck, replan). All of this runs without interruption.
steps.student_prompt: Document your pipeline architecture with data flow between each stage.

steps.tutor_message: Run 5 trials on a freshly randomized course (cones moved between trials). Record: completion %, mean time, score. Your system should handle course variation without re-programming.
steps.student_prompt: Run 5 autonomous trials with randomized cone positions each time.

steps.tutor_message: Calculate robustness metrics: how sensitive is completion rate to cone position variation? What's the tightest course layout that still works? This analysis defines your system's operational design domain (ODD).
steps.reflection_question: What's your system's biggest single point of failure? If you had one more day to fix it, what would you change?

completion_badge.name: Autonomous Champion
completion_badge.description: Completed the full gauntlet with zero human input

challenges.title: Cone SLAM
challenges.subtitle: Map cone positions as dynamic landmarks
challenges.description: Treat cones as SLAM landmarks: build a map of cone positions while simultaneously localizing the robot. The cone map persists across the full run.
learning_objectives: Extend SLAM to use detected objects as landmarks
Handle landmark initialization and data association
Maintain a consistent map under repeated observations

steps.tutor_message: In cone SLAM, each detected cone is a landmark. On first detection: initialize a new landmark with estimated world position (from depth + heading). On subsequent detection: update the landmark's position using EKF. Track landmark ID across frames using color + position.
steps.reflection_question: Two orange cones look identical. How do you solve the data association problem — knowing that observation #7 belongs to landmark #3, not #5?

steps.tutor_message: Implement a simplified cone SLAM: maintain a dictionary {cone_id: (x, y, color)}. On detection, find the nearest known cone by position and color. If distance < threshold → update. Else → new landmark. Track uncertainty with a covariance estimate.
steps.student_prompt: Implement cone SLAM and visualize the landmark map at the end of a full course run.

steps.tutor_message: Loop closure: when the robot returns near a known cone and re-detects it, the position error between expected and observed provides a correction for all recent poses. This is SLAM's loop-closure benefit applied to cone landmarks.
steps.reflection_question: How many unique cones are needed as landmarks for reliable localization? What's the minimum spatial coverage?

completion_badge.name: Cone SLAM Master
completion_badge.description: Mapped cone positions as SLAM landmarks

packs.name: Computer Vision
packs.description: From pixels to perception — learn how cameras see the world and make robots react to what they see.

challenges.title: What Is a Pixel?
challenges.subtitle: Images are grids of numbers
challenges.description: Before computers can 'see', we need to understand what a digital image actually is: a grid of numbers. Each number is a pixel. Change the numbers, change the picture.
learning_objectives: Understand that images are 2D arrays of numbers
Interpret pixel values as brightness or color
Recognize the tradeoff between resolution and file size

steps.tutor_message: A grayscale image is just a grid of numbers from 0 (black) to 255 (white). A 4×4 image has 16 pixels — 16 numbers. A 4K camera has 3840×2160 = 8,294,400 pixels per frame. Same idea, way more numbers.
steps.reflection_question: If each pixel uses 1 byte, how many bytes is one 4K grayscale frame? How about one second of 4K video at 30fps?

steps.tutor_message: I'm going to draw a 16×16 pixel grid where each cell shows its brightness value. Dark cells get dense hatching; light cells stay mostly white.

payload.prompt: a 16x16 pixel grid where each cell is filled with hatching density proportional to a simple gradient: bright top-left, dark bottom-right. Label the corner values: 255 top-left, 0 bottom-right

steps.tutor_message: Color images have three grids (channels): Red, Green, Blue. Each pixel is three numbers. (255, 0, 0) = pure red. (0, 255, 0) = pure green. (128, 0, 128) = purple (red + blue mixed). Mixing light is different from mixing paint!
steps.reflection_question: What RGB values give you pure yellow? (Hint: yellow light = red + green light)

completion_badge.name: Pixel Decoder
completion_badge.description: Understood images as arrays of numbers

challenges.title: Color Hunt
challenges.subtitle: Find all the red things in the frame
challenges.description: Color detection is the simplest computer vision technique — and one of the most useful for robotics. Write a detector that finds objects of a specific color.
learning_objectives: Implement HSV color thresholding
Find connected components in a binary mask
Understand why HSV is better than RGB for color detection

steps.tutor_message: RGB changes with lighting: a red ball in shadow has low R, G, B values. In HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value), the Hue stays constant across lighting — only Value changes. That's why robotics uses HSV for color detection.
steps.student_prompt: Open the camera feed. Convert the frame to HSV using your code editor.

steps.tutor_message: Apply an HSV threshold for red: H=0–10° or H=170–180° (red wraps around). S>100, V>50. The output is a binary mask: 1 where the color matches, 0 elsewhere. Any white blobs in the mask are candidate red objects.
steps.student_prompt: Write the HSV threshold and display the resulting binary mask.

steps.tutor_message: Use cv2.findContours to find blobs in the mask. Filter by area (>500px²). Draw a bounding box around the largest blob. Test it: hold a red object in front of the camera. Does it detect it reliably?
steps.reflection_question: Red wraps around in HSV (0° and 360° are both red). How do you threshold for a color that spans the H=0 boundary?

completion_badge.name: Color Finder
completion_badge.description: Detected colored objects using HSV thresholding

challenges.title: Shape Spotter
challenges.subtitle: Find circles and rectangles in images
challenges.description: Geometric shape detection is everywhere: QR codes (squares), stop signs (octagons), wheels (circles). Learn to detect them using contour analysis.
learning_objectives: Apply edge detection (Canny) to find shape boundaries
Approximate contours as polygons
Classify shapes by vertex count and circularity

steps.tutor_message: Edge detection finds the boundaries of shapes. Canny edge detector: blur the image → compute gradient → threshold strong vs weak edges. The result is a binary image where white lines = object boundaries.
steps.student_prompt: Apply cv2.Canny to a camera frame and display the edge image.

steps.tutor_message: cv2.findContours finds closed loops in the edge image. cv2.approxPolyDP approximates each contour as a polygon. Count vertices: 3=triangle, 4=rectangle, >10=circle. A circle has circularity = 4π×area/perimeter² ≈ 1.0.
steps.student_prompt: Find contours, classify by vertex count and circularity, draw bounding shapes.

steps.tutor_message: Place a circle, a rectangle, and a triangle in front of the camera. Does your detector correctly classify all three? What breaks the classifier?
steps.reflection_question: A crumpled piece of paper has lots of irregular contours. How do you filter out noise and keep only the 'real' shapes?

completion_badge.name: Shape Finder
completion_badge.description: Detected and classified geometric shapes

challenges.title: AprilTag Magic
challenges.subtitle: How fiducial markers enable precise localization
challenges.description: SketchBot uses AprilTags to know exactly where the paper is. Learn what makes these markers so reliable and how they encode both identity and pose.
learning_objectives: Understand how AprilTags encode ID in a binary matrix
Detect AprilTags and extract their 4-corner positions
Compute camera-to-tag pose using corner correspondences

steps.tutor_message: An AprilTag is a black-and-white binary matrix inside a black border. Each cell is 1 bit. A 6×6 interior = 36 bits → 2^36 unique IDs. The black border makes detection easy (high contrast); error-correcting codes make it robust to partial occlusion.
steps.reflection_question: Why is error correction important for a tag that might be seen from 45°, partially blocked, or under harsh lighting?

steps.tutor_message: Using the apriltag library: detector = apriltag.Detector(). results = detector.detect(gray_image). Each result has: id, corners (4×2 array), pose (rotation + translation). The corners are the key data — they give you everything.
steps.student_prompt: Detect AprilTags in the SketchBot camera feed. Print detected IDs and corner positions.

steps.tutor_message: From 4 known 3D corners and their 2D image positions, you can compute the camera's exact 3D pose relative to the tag using PnP. This is how SketchBot knows the paper's position even if the camera moves.
steps.reflection_question: If one corner of an AprilTag is occluded, can you still compute the pose? What's the minimum number of visible corners needed?

completion_badge.name: Tag Detector
completion_badge.description: Detected AprilTags and extracted their 3D pose

challenges.title: Motion Detector
challenges.subtitle: Detect movement between frames
challenges.description: Security cameras detect motion by comparing consecutive frames. Pixels that change significantly between frames are 'moving'. Implement a basic motion detector.
learning_objectives: Compute frame difference to detect motion
Threshold and morphologically clean motion regions
Distinguish real motion from camera noise

steps.tutor_message: Frame difference: diff = |frame_t - frame_(t-1)|. High values in diff = pixels that changed = motion. Threshold the diff to get a binary mask. Each white region is a moving object.
steps.student_prompt: Implement frame differencing and display the motion mask.

steps.tutor_message: Camera noise causes some pixels to randomly change even in a static scene. Fix: apply Gaussian blur before differencing. Also use morphological operations (cv2.dilate + cv2.erode) to fill holes and remove tiny speckles.
steps.student_prompt: Add blur and morphological cleanup to reduce false positives.

steps.tutor_message: Test: walk past the camera slowly, then quickly. How does motion speed affect the diff magnitude? Try moving only one finger — does the detector catch it?
steps.reflection_question: Frame differencing can't detect a stationary person who just stopped moving. What technique would detect them?

completion_badge.name: Motion Watcher
completion_badge.description: Built a frame-differencing motion detector

challenges.title: Edge Finder
challenges.subtitle: Detect and visualize image edges
challenges.description: Edges are where intensity changes sharply — object boundaries, shadows, and texture transitions. Visualize how different edge detectors work and what each reveals.
learning_objectives: Understand gradient-based edge detection
Compare Sobel, Laplacian, and Canny operators
Tune edge detector thresholds for different images

steps.tutor_message: An edge = a sudden change in brightness. The gradient measures this: high gradient = edge. Sobel computes the gradient in X and Y separately; combine as sqrt(Gx² + Gy²). Laplacian uses a second derivative — it finds edges from both sides simultaneously.
steps.student_prompt: Apply Sobel X, Sobel Y, and combined Sobel to the camera image. Display all three.

steps.tutor_message: Canny is the gold standard: blur → Sobel → non-maximum suppression (thin lines to 1 pixel) → hysteresis thresholding (strong edges keep, weak edges kept only if connected to strong). Result: clean, thin edge lines.
steps.student_prompt: Apply cv2.Canny and compare to raw Sobel. Which looks cleaner?

steps.tutor_message: Have SketchBot draw the Canny edges of an object — essentially an automatic sketch generator. The robot traces the edge map from the camera onto paper.

payload.prompt: an abstract edge-map sketch: strong geometric lines and curves representing the detected edges of an everyday object (mug, book, phone), line art only, no fills

completion_badge.name: Edge Analyst
completion_badge.description: Implemented and compared multiple edge detection operators

challenges.title: Image Classifier
challenges.subtitle: Build a simple object classifier
challenges.description: Train a classifier to distinguish between 3 object categories using feature extraction and a simple ML model. No deep learning — just features and a decision boundary.
learning_objectives: Extract color histogram and shape features from images
Train a k-NN or SVM classifier
Evaluate with precision, recall, and confusion matrix

steps.tutor_message: Classical CV uses hand-crafted features: color histograms (distribution of pixel values), HOG (histogram of oriented gradients for shape), and area/perimeter ratios. These turn an image into a fixed-length vector a classifier can use.
steps.student_prompt: Choose 3 object categories and collect 30 images each. Extract HSV color histograms as features.

steps.tutor_message: Train a k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) classifier on your features. Split 80/20 train/test. Vary k from 1 to 11 and plot accuracy vs k. Find the optimal k.
steps.student_prompt: Train k-NN and plot accuracy vs k.

steps.tutor_message: Compute the confusion matrix: a 3×3 table showing predicted vs actual class for every test sample. Which class is most confused with which? What feature would help separate them?
steps.reflection_question: Your classifier is 90% accurate. But if 90% of your samples are one class, a dummy classifier that always predicts that class is also 90%. How do you detect this 'accuracy paradox'?

completion_badge.name: Object Classifier
completion_badge.description: Built and evaluated a traditional image classifier

challenges.title: Optical Flow
challenges.subtitle: Track motion between frames
challenges.description: Optical flow estimates how each pixel moved between frames — a dense velocity field. Sparse optical flow tracks specific keypoints. Both are fundamental for robotics.
learning_objectives: Implement Lucas-Kanade sparse optical flow
Visualize flow vectors as arrows on the image
Apply optical flow to robot velocity estimation

steps.tutor_message: Lucas-Kanade optical flow assumes: between two frames, a small patch of pixels moves rigidly. It solves for the (vx, vy) velocity that best explains the brightness change. It works only for small displacements — fast motion needs a pyramid (multi-scale).
steps.reflection_question: What assumption does LK flow make that breaks in a scene with multiple independently moving objects?

steps.tutor_message: Implement: detect corners with cv2.goodFeaturesToTrack. Track them in the next frame with cv2.calcOpticalFlowPyrLK. Draw arrows from old position to new position for each tracked point.
steps.student_prompt: Implement sparse optical flow and visualize flow arrows on the camera feed.

steps.tutor_message: When the camera moves (robot drives), ALL tracked points move in the same direction — this is ego-motion. Average the flow vectors to estimate the robot's camera velocity. Compare to wheel encoder estimates.
steps.reflection_question: How do you separate ego-motion (camera moving) from scene motion (objects moving) using only optical flow?

completion_badge.name: Flow Tracker
completion_badge.description: Implemented optical flow and estimated camera motion

challenges.title: Color Segmentation
challenges.subtitle: Separate objects by color cluster
challenges.description: K-means clustering groups pixels by color similarity — no labels needed. The result is an automatic segmentation of the image into color regions.
learning_objectives: Apply k-means clustering to image pixels
Interpret cluster centroids as dominant colors
Use segmentation for object isolation

steps.tutor_message: k-means: initialize k cluster centers randomly. Assign each pixel to the nearest center. Recompute centers as the mean of assigned pixels. Repeat until stable. For k=5, the result is an image with only 5 colors — one per cluster.
steps.reflection_question: How do you choose k? What visual artifact appears when k is too small vs too large?

steps.tutor_message: Reshape the image to (N_pixels, 3). Apply cv2.kmeans with k=5. Reshape back and display. Each pixel now shows its cluster's color. Objects with distinct colors become clearly separated regions.
steps.student_prompt: Implement k-means color segmentation and display the result for k=3, 5, and 8.

steps.tutor_message: Select one cluster (by clicking on the dominant color of an object). Create a mask of all pixels in that cluster. This isolates the object — even in a complex scene with no labeled training data.
steps.reflection_question: K-means is sensitive to initialization — different runs give different results. How do you make segmentation deterministic (same result every run)?

completion_badge.name: Segmentation Artist
completion_badge.description: Segmented an image using k-means clustering

challenges.title: Face Detection
challenges.subtitle: Find faces using Haar cascades
challenges.description: Haar cascade classifiers were the state-of-the-art face detector in 2001 — and they still work well today. Learn the Viola-Jones algorithm and its clever integral image optimization.
learning_objectives: Understand Haar features and cascade architecture
Use the integral image for O(1) feature computation
Detect faces at multiple scales using image pyramids

steps.tutor_message: Haar features are rectangle patterns: 'dark region to the left of a bright region'. A face has many such patterns: dark eye sockets below a bright forehead, dark nostrils in a bright nose. A cascade of 6000 such features is checked at every position/scale.
steps.reflection_question: Why does the cascade architecture make detection fast? (Hint: think about how many positions are rejected at the first few features vs the last)

steps.tutor_message: The integral image: I(x,y) = sum of all pixels above and left of (x,y). Pre-computed once. Any rectangular sum is then computed in exactly 4 operations, regardless of rectangle size. This makes Haar features O(1) to evaluate.
steps.student_prompt: Compute the integral image of a 4×4 test matrix by hand. Verify a sum query using the formula.

steps.tutor_message: Load OpenCV's pre-trained Haar cascade. Apply to the camera feed. Tune scaleFactor and minNeighbors to balance detection rate vs false positives.
steps.student_prompt: Detect faces live from the camera. Find settings that minimize false positives.
steps.reflection_question: Haar cascade was trained on frontal faces. What happens when you turn your head 45°? What approach would handle arbitrary angles?

completion_badge.name: Face Finder
completion_badge.description: Implemented Haar cascade face detection

challenges.title: Camera Calibration
challenges.subtitle: Remove lens distortion
challenges.description: Every camera lens distorts the image — straight lines look curved near the edges. Calibration finds the distortion parameters and corrects them.
learning_objectives: Understand intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters
Collect calibration images using a chessboard pattern
Apply undistortion and verify with straight-line objects

steps.tutor_message: Camera distortion types: radial (barrel = lines bow outward, pincushion = bow inward) and tangential (lens not perfectly parallel to sensor). A checkerboard pattern has perfectly straight lines — any curve in the image is pure distortion.
steps.reflection_question: Why is a checkerboard ideal for calibration? What property of the grid makes corner detection easy and reliable?

steps.tutor_message: Collect 20 images of the checkerboard at different angles and distances. OpenCV finds the checkerboard corners in each image automatically. More images from more angles = better calibration.
steps.student_prompt: Collect 20 calibration images and run cv2.calibrateCamera. Report the reprojection error.

steps.tutor_message: Apply cv2.undistort to a live camera frame. Find a straight edge (a door frame, a ruler) and compare distorted vs undistorted. Reprojection error < 1 pixel = good calibration.
steps.reflection_question: After undistortion, the image has black borders at the edges. Why? How do you crop them out?

completion_badge.name: Calibration Expert
completion_badge.description: Calibrated a camera and removed lens distortion

challenges.title: YOLO in Action
challenges.subtitle: Real-time object detection with YOLO
challenges.description: YOLO (You Only Look Once) processes the entire image in one neural network pass — making it fast enough for real-time robotics. Run YOLO on the robot's camera.
learning_objectives: Understand the YOLO grid-based detection architecture
Run YOLO inference on live video
Interpret confidence scores and NMS suppression

steps.tutor_message: YOLO divides the image into an S×S grid. Each cell predicts B bounding boxes and class probabilities. A single forward pass produces all detections simultaneously — unlike sliding window methods that run the network hundreds of times per frame.
steps.reflection_question: YOLO struggles with small objects clustered together. Why does the grid architecture cause this? Which YOLO version addressed it?

steps.tutor_message: Run YOLOv8n (nano — fastest) on the camera feed using Ultralytics. Display bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores. Measure FPS on the Pi/laptop.
steps.student_prompt: Run YOLOv8n on live camera and report detected classes and FPS.

steps.tutor_message: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS): YOLO outputs dozens of overlapping boxes per object. NMS keeps only the highest-confidence box when boxes overlap > IoU threshold. Tweak the IoU threshold and watch how it affects duplicate detections.
steps.reflection_question: When would you LOWER the IoU threshold? When would you raise it? What's the effect on false positives vs missed detections?

completion_badge.name: YOLO Runner
completion_badge.description: Ran real-time YOLO detection on robot camera

challenges.title: Homography Transform
challenges.subtitle: Warp camera view to top-down
challenges.description: A homography maps one plane to another — transform the angled camera view into a flat top-down map. Essential for SketchBot's coordinate system.
learning_objectives: Understand homography as a projective transformation
Compute homography from 4 point correspondences
Apply warpPerspective to create a top-down view

steps.tutor_message: A homography is a 3×3 matrix that maps points from one plane to another via: [x', y', w']^T = H × [x, y, 1]^T. Four point correspondences uniquely determine H. This is exactly how SketchBot converts camera coordinates to paper coordinates.
steps.reflection_question: Why do you need exactly 4 points (not 3 or 5) to uniquely determine a homography?

steps.tutor_message: Click 4 corners of the paper in the camera image. Specify their desired positions in the output (flat map). cv2.getPerspectiveTransform → homography matrix. cv2.warpPerspective → top-down image.
steps.student_prompt: Implement the paper-to-top-down transform using the 4 AprilTag corners.

steps.tutor_message: Place a ruler on the paper at a known angle. In the warped top-down view, the ruler should appear exactly horizontal or vertical. Pixel distance in the top-down view should map linearly to real-world cm.
steps.reflection_question: The homography assumes the paper is perfectly flat. What happens to the transform if the paper has a slight curve?

completion_badge.name: Perspective Warper
completion_badge.description: Computed and applied a homography transform

challenges.title: Stereo Vision
challenges.subtitle: Depth from two cameras
challenges.description: Two cameras separated by a known baseline give depth from disparity — the same way human eyes work. Implement a stereo depth pipeline.
learning_objectives: Understand the epipolar constraint
Compute disparity maps using block matching
Convert disparity to metric depth using the stereo geometry formula

steps.tutor_message: Two cameras, baseline b apart, see the same point at horizontal offsets x_L and x_R in their images. Disparity d = x_L - x_R. Depth Z = f × b / d, where f is focal length. Far objects have small disparity; close objects have large disparity.
steps.reflection_question: A point directly at infinity has d=0. What does that mean for depth estimation? What's the maximum measurable depth?

steps.tutor_message: Stereo calibration: find the rotation and translation between both cameras. Use cv2.stereoCalibrate with paired checkerboard images. Then cv2.stereoRectify to align the two image planes to the same horizontal epipolar lines.
steps.student_prompt: Calibrate two cameras stereo and verify rectification by checking that epipolar lines are horizontal.

steps.tutor_message: Compute the disparity map: cv2.StereoBM or cv2.StereoSGBM. Convert to depth: depth = f × b / disparity. Visualize as a color-coded depth map. Measure a known object and compare estimated vs actual depth.
steps.reflection_question: SGBM is slower but smoother than BM. In which robotics application would each be preferred?

completion_badge.name: Depth Perceiver
completion_badge.description: Built a stereo vision depth pipeline

challenges.title: Visual Servoing
challenges.subtitle: Drive the robot using camera feedback
challenges.description: Visual servoing closes the loop between what the camera sees and how the robot moves. Track a target feature and use its position error to generate motor commands.
learning_objectives: Implement image-based visual servo (IBVS)
Define image features and compute error
Design a stable feedback gain that avoids oscillation

steps.tutor_message: Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS): define desired image features s* (e.g., target marker at image center). Measure current features s. Error e = s - s*. Control law: ΔV = -λ L_s⁺ e, where L_s is the image Jacobian. The robot moves to minimize e.
steps.reflection_question: Why might a large gain λ cause oscillation even though it reduces error faster?

steps.tutor_message: Simplified IBVS: detect target centroid. Error = (centroid_x - image_center_x, centroid_y - image_center_y). Linear velocity ∝ -error_y. Angular velocity ∝ -error_x. The robot turns to center the target and drives toward it.
steps.student_prompt: Implement simplified IBVS to track a red target and drive toward it.

steps.tutor_message: Test stability: disturb the robot by hand during tracking. Does it recover? Increase λ until oscillation appears, then halve it. Plot the error signal over time — it should decay exponentially for a stable servo.
steps.reflection_question: Full IBVS requires computing the image Jacobian L_s — a 6×6 matrix relating feature motion to camera velocity. Why is this harder than the simplified version? What does it enable?

completion_badge.name: Visual Servo Engineer
completion_badge.description: Closed the loop between camera and robot motion

challenges.title: Train Your Own Model
challenges.subtitle: Custom neural network for robot perception
challenges.description: Train a convolutional neural network (CNN) from scratch on a custom dataset relevant to your robot's task. Understand why architecture choices matter.
learning_objectives: Design a CNN architecture for a specific task
Apply data augmentation to improve generalization
Analyze overfitting and apply regularization techniques

steps.tutor_message: A CNN architecture: INPUT → CONV → RELU → POOL → CONV → RELU → POOL → FLATTEN → DENSE → OUTPUT. Each CONV layer learns spatial features at increasing abstraction. Deeper networks learn more complex features but need more data.
steps.student_prompt: Design a CNN architecture for classifying the objects your robot encounters. Justify your layer choices.

steps.tutor_message: Data augmentation artificially increases dataset size: random flips, rotations (±15°), brightness jitter (±30%), random crop. For robotics: also add simulated shadows and motion blur. A model trained with augmentation generalizes far better.
steps.student_prompt: Implement augmentation and train the CNN. Compare validation accuracy with and without augmentation.

steps.tutor_message: Overfitting: training accuracy >> validation accuracy. The model memorized the training data. Fixes: add dropout (randomly zero neurons during training), reduce model size, add L2 regularization, or collect more data.
steps.reflection_question: Plot training vs validation loss over 50 epochs. Identify the exact epoch where overfitting begins. Why stop training there?

completion_badge.name: Model Trainer
completion_badge.description: Trained a custom CNN for robot perception

challenges.title: SketchBot Vision OS
challenges.subtitle: A complete computer vision pipeline for SketchBot
challenges.description: Build the complete perception stack for SketchBot: calibration, AprilTag localization, drawing surface detection, line tracking, and quality verification — all running in one pipeline.
learning_objectives: Integrate multiple CV subsystems into one production pipeline
Handle failure modes and fallbacks in each subsystem
Measure end-to-end latency and optimize the bottleneck

steps.tutor_message: The full SketchBot vision pipeline: FRAME CAPTURE → UNDISTORT → APRILTAG DETECTION → HOMOGRAPHY UPDATE → DRAWING SURFACE MASK → LINE QUALITY CHECK → PUBLISH STATE. Each stage runs at a different frequency. Design the pipeline with parallelism in mind.
steps.student_prompt: Design the pipeline architecture: stage order, frequencies, and data handoff.

steps.tutor_message: Implement each stage as a thread or async coroutine. Use a shared state dict protected by a lock. Measure the latency of each stage — find the bottleneck. The slowest stage limits the whole pipeline.
steps.student_prompt: Implement the full pipeline with threading and profile each stage's latency.

steps.tutor_message: Add failure fallbacks: if AprilTags are not detected (occluded), hold the last known homography. If the drawing surface mask fails, use the full image. If frame capture drops, retry 3 times then alarm. Robust pipelines fail gracefully.
steps.reflection_question: What's the maximum tolerable pipeline latency for SketchBot to draw accurately at its operating speed? How did your implemented pipeline compare?

completion_badge.name: Vision Architect
completion_badge.description: Built the complete SketchBot CV perception pipeline
